proposals.title: Host c-kitW41 Mutation Alters the Bone Marrow Niche and Disease Phenotype of Engrafted Human Myelodysplastic Syndrome Cells
proposals.motivation: Humanized mouse models, such as the MISTRG platform, are critical for studying human hematologic malignancies like Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS). However, the MISTRG model carries a host c-kitW41 mutation to enhance human hematopoietic stem cell engraftment. Given that c-kit signaling is central to hematopoietic stem cell niche interactions, this mutation may inadvertently alter the pathophysiology of engrafted human MDS cells, confounding the interpretation of disease mechanisms and therapy responses derived from these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the c-kitW41 mutation in the host mouse bone marrow niche fundamentally alters the transcriptional program, clonal dynamics, and disease phenotype of engrafted primary human MDS cells compared to their behavior in a wild-type c-kit environment.
proposals.method: We will engraft primary human MDS patient samples into two parallel humanized mouse models: the standard MISTRG (c-kitW41/W41) and a newly engineered MISTRG variant with a wild-type c-kit locus. Using longitudinal flow cytometry, we will track human hematopoietic and MDS-specific cell populations. We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on engrafted human cells and mouse stromal cells to dissect cell-intrinsic and niche-mediated transcriptional changes. Computational analysis will involve differential expression (DESeq2, Seurat) and cell-cell communication inference (CellChat) to map altered signaling pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific confounding effects of the c-kitW41 mutation on human MDS modeling, providing a critical framework for interpreting preclinical data from widely used humanized models. It will identify niche-dependent vulnerabilities in MDS cells, potentially revealing novel therapeutic targets that are dependent on specific stromal interactions.
proposals.novelty: While humanized mice are extensively used, a systematic investigation into how the foundational c-kit mutation—engineered to facilitate humanization—itself skews the disease phenotype of a human myeloid malignancy has not been conducted. This directly addresses a potential blind spot in translational MDS research using these models.

proposals.title: Human Cytokine Augmentation in Humanized Mice: A Critical Evaluation of TPO and GM-CSF for Improved Myeloid and Hematopoietic Stem Cell Compartment Reconstitution
proposals.motivation: Humanized mouse models, such as NSG-SGM3 mice expressing human SCF, GM-CSF, and IL-3, have revolutionized the study of human immune responses in vivo. However, pre-2026 studies indicate persistent deficiencies in human myeloid cell development, hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) maintenance, and platelet reconstitution, limiting their utility for modeling diseases like cancer and chronic inflammation where these compartments are critical. This gap suggests that the current cytokine milieu may be insufficient for optimal human immune system engraftment and function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the engraftment, maintenance, and function of human immune cells, particularly myeloid lineages and HSCs, in humanized mice will be significantly enhanced by the additional knock-in of human thrombopoietin (TPO) and/or granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) genes, beyond the standard cytokine set.
proposals.method: We will engineer novel NOD-scid IL2Rγnull (NSG) mouse strains using CRISPR-Cas9 to knock in human TPO and GM-CSF genes under murine regulatory elements, creating single and double transgenic lines. These strains will be engrafted with human CD34+ hematopoietic stem cells from multiple donors. Engraftment will be longitudinally assessed via flow cytometry for lymphoid and myeloid subsets, histological analysis of bone marrow and spleen, and functional assays for platelet production and myeloid cell responses to pathogens. The performance will be benchmarked against standard NSG and NSG-SGM3 mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a next-generation humanized mouse model with superior human myeloid and stem cell reconstitution, directly addressing a major limitation in the field. It will provide a more physiologically relevant platform for pre-clinical testing in oncology (e.g., for myeloid-targeting therapies) and for studying human-specific aspects of hematopoiesis and inflammatory skin diseases like atopic dermatitis in vivo.
proposals.novelty: While existing models add cytokines like SCF, GM-CSF, and IL-3, the specific and combined role of human TPO (for HSC/platelets) and endogenous, physiologically expressed GM-CSF in improving overall human immune system architecture in vivo remains untested and non-obvious, moving beyond mere lymphoid focus to holistic immune system engineering.

proposals.title: The EGFR-STAT1 Axis as a Sustained Driver of Fibrosis: A Therapeutic Target for Established Disease
proposals.motivation: Fibrosis is a pathological hallmark of numerous chronic diseases, including systemic sclerosis and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, leading to organ failure. While EGFR and STAT1 signaling have been implicated in fibrosis initiation, it remains unknown if this pathway is required for the maintenance of established fibrotic lesions, a critical distinction for therapeutic intervention. This gap limits the development of targeted anti-fibrotic strategies for patients with ongoing disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the EGFR-STAT1 signaling axis is not only required for the initiation of fibrosis but is also a sustained, non-redundant driver necessary for the maintenance of established fibrotic tissue.
proposals.method: We will employ a genetic, inducible mouse model of skin fibrosis (e.g., bleomycin-induced or TSK-1 model) combined with conditional, tamoxifen-inducible Cre-lox systems to delete Egfr or Stat1 specifically in fibroblasts (using Col1a2-CreER). Deletion will be timed either at fibrosis induction (initiation) or after fibrosis is fully established (maintenance). Fibrosis will be quantified by histology (Masson's trichrome), hydroxyproline content, and single-cell RNA sequencing of skin lesions to define fibroblast activation states and pathway dependency. Parallel studies will use primary human dermal fibroblasts from fibrotic lesions in 3D collagen matrices, treated with EGFR (gefitinib) or STAT1 (fludarabine) inhibitors at different timepoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether EGFR-STAT1 is a 'maintenance pathway' for fibrosis, shifting the paradigm from targeting initiation to targeting sustained disease. It will provide a direct preclinical rationale for repurposing EGFR/STAT1 inhibitors in patients with established fibrotic disorders. The integrated human-mouse approach aligns with Flavell's focus on human immunology and disease mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While EGFR-STAT1's role in initiating fibroblast activation is recognized, its continuous requirement in maintaining the fibrotic extracellular matrix in vivo is untested and non-obvious, as the lesion may become self-sustaining or switch to alternative pathways.

proposals.title: Targeting the EGFR-STAT1 Axis to Reprogram Fibroblast Activation and Promote Scarless Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: Pathological scarring, including hypertrophic scars and keloids, represents a major clinical burden with significant functional and psychosocial consequences. Current treatments are largely empirical and often ineffective. Pre-2026 research implicates dysregulated EGFR signaling in excessive fibroblast proliferation and a pro-fibrotic STAT1-driven inflammatory response as key drivers of scar formation, but their coordinated targeting in wound healing remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined, localized inhibition of EGFR and STAT1 signaling during the proliferative phase of wound healing will reprogram dermal fibroblast function, shifting them from a pro-fibrotic to a regenerative phenotype, thereby accelerating re-epithelialization while minimizing collagen deposition and scar formation.
proposals.method: We will employ a humanized mouse model of dermal wound healing, leveraging Flavell's expertise in engineering mice to study human immunity and skin biology. Full-thickness excisional wounds will be treated topically with a hydrogel co-delivering the EGFR inhibitor gefitinib and the STAT1 inhibitor fludarabine. Wound closure kinetics will be tracked longitudinally. At defined timepoints, wounds will be harvested for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of the healing tissue to map cellular heterogeneity and fibroblast activation states. Bulk RNA-seq and protein analysis (western blot, multiplex cytokine assays) will validate pathway inhibition and define the molecular signature of the healed tissue. Scar morphology will be assessed histologically (H&E, Masson's trichrome) for collagen architecture and epidermal maturation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, mechanism-based combinatorial therapy for scar reduction. It will provide a high-resolution, single-cell atlas of fibroblast plasticity during wound healing under targeted pathway inhibition, defining a pro-regenerative gene signature. The findings could directly translate into a new topical treatment strategy for improving wound outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While EGFR or STAT1 modulation has been studied individually in fibrosis, their concurrent inhibition to specifically uncouple wound closure from scarring is a novel therapeutic concept. The use of a humanized mouse model and scRNA-seq analysis to deconvolve the fibroblast response to this dual targeting provides a uniquely powerful and translational approach not previously applied to this problem.

proposals.title: The Mitochondrial Protein MCJ is a Metabolic Checkpoint for CD8+ T Cell Effector Differentiation and Anti-Tumor Function
proposals.motivation: T cell differentiation into effector and memory subsets is tightly linked to metabolic reprogramming, particularly a shift to oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) for memory formation. MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein) is an endogenous negative regulator of mitochondrial complex I, known to restrain OXPHOS in hepatocytes and influence CD8+ T cell metabolism in cancer models. Its precise, cell-intrinsic role in dictating T cell fate decisions during an immune response remains undefined, representing a critical gap in linking mitochondrial regulation to immune function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MCJ acts as a required metabolic brake in CD8+ T cells, where its expression is necessary to limit effector differentiation and promote the development of long-lived, OXPHOS-competent memory precursors.
proposals.method: We will employ MCJ-floxed mice crossed to Cd4-Cre for T cell-specific deletion. Using the 'Engineering Mice to Study Human Immunity' approach, we will adoptively transfer WT vs. MCJ-deficient OT-I T cells into hosts and challenge with OVA-expressing tumor or Listeria models. We will analyze differentiation (flow cytometry for T-bet, Eomes, KLRG1, CD127), function (cytokine production, cytotoxicity), and persistence. Metabolic profiling (Seahorse, mitochondrial membrane potential) will be integrated with RNA-seq and ATAC-seq on sorted subsets to define transcriptional and epigenetic landscapes. PRMT activity, given its role in methylation and Flavell's expertise, will be assessed for potential regulation of MCJ.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish MCJ as a novel mitochondrial checkpoint protein that actively shapes T cell fate by coupling metabolic restraint to transcriptional programs. It will provide mechanistic insight into how intrinsic metabolic regulators determine the balance between short-lived effectors and long-lived memory cells, with direct implications for improving T cell-based cancer immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in metabolism is known, its function as a *necessary regulator specifically for T cell differentiation* is unexplored. This proposal uniquely positions MCJ not just as a metabolic modulator, but as a decisive fate-determining factor, integrating immunology with mitochondrial biology in a novel way.

proposals.title: Enhancing T Cell Therapy Efficacy and Persistence through MCJ Deletion in Engineered Human T Cells for Cancer and Autoimmunity
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T cell therapies, including TCR-T cells and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), face limitations due to T cell exhaustion and metabolic insufficiency in the tumor microenvironment. Pre-2026 work identified MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) as a mitochondrial brake; its deletion in mouse CD8+ T cells enhanced anti-tumor activity and memory formation. This proposal aims to translate this finding to human T cell engineering, a core theme of Flavell's lab, to overcome a key barrier in cellular immunotherapy for cancer and potentially repurpose these cells for autoimmune disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic deletion of MCJ in human TCR-engineered T cells and expanded TILs will enhance their mitochondrial fitness, resistance to exhaustion, and long-term persistence, thereby improving therapeutic efficacy in solid tumors and enabling novel applications in autoimmune disease settings.
proposals.method: We will engineer human T cells from healthy donors and cancer patients using CRISPR-Cas9 to knock out MCJ. These cells will be further modified with tumor-antigen-specific TCRs or expanded as TILs. Using pre-2026 metabolic assays (Seahorse analyzer), transcriptomics (single-cell RNA-seq), and epigenetic profiling (ATAC-seq), we will assess mitochondrial respiration, exhaustion markers (e.g., TOX, PD-1), and chromatin accessibility. Functional efficacy will be tested in vitro against tumor organoids and in vivo using established humanized mouse models of melanoma and lung cancer. A parallel arm will test MCJ-deleted regulatory T cells (Tregs) in a humanized mouse model of graft-versus-host disease.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish MCJ as a novel, druggable target to potentiate human T cell therapies, providing a universal metabolic engineering strategy applicable to both TCR-T and TIL platforms. It will deliver a comprehensive pre-clinical dataset on the molecular and functional consequences of MCJ loss in human T cells, bridging immunometabolism and translational immunotherapy. Furthermore, it will pioneer the concept of metabolically enhancing Tregs for treating inflammatory diseases.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in mouse T cells is known, its functional impact in engineered *human* T cells for therapy is unexplored. Extending the target from chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cells (as in prior studies) to the more physiologically nuanced TCR-T and TIL platforms, and exploring its application in autoimmunity, represents a significant conceptual and translational advance.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling Reveals MCJ as a Metabolic Rheostat for T Cell Heterogeneity and Exhaustion
proposals.motivation: T cell exhaustion is a major barrier to effective anti-tumor immunity and chronic infection clearance. Pre-2026 work has identified Mitochondrial Coiled-coil protein J (MCJ) as a negative regulator of mitochondrial respiration in T cells, with its deletion enhancing anti-tumor responses in mice. However, the precise transcriptional and functional heterogeneity of MCJ-modulated T cell populations remains unresolved, limiting our ability to harness this pathway therapeutically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MCJ deletion remodels the transcriptional landscape of tumor-infiltrating T cells, reducing the heterogeneity of exhausted subsets and promoting the emergence of metabolically fit, stem-like or effector subpopulations that underpin superior anti-tumor function.
proposals.method: We will employ droplet-based single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics Chromium) on CD8+ T cells sorted from MCJ-deficient and wild-type mice bearing B16 melanoma or MC38 colon adenocarcinoma tumors. Data will be processed using Cell Ranger and analyzed with Seurat (v4) for clustering, differential expression, and trajectory inference (Monocle3). We will integrate our dataset with pre-2026 human T cell exhaustion atlases to identify conserved MCJ-associated modules. Metabolic flux analysis will validate key transcriptional predictions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of how mitochondrial metabolic regulation by MCJ shapes T cell fate and heterogeneity in the tumor microenvironment. It will identify novel transcriptional signatures and candidate surface markers for metabolically enhanced T cells, informing strategies to improve adoptive cell therapies and checkpoint blockade.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in T cell metabolism is known, applying single-cell transcriptomics to this system is novel and will move beyond bulk measurements to uncover the specific subpopulations and transitional states that are most critically regulated by mitochondrial restraint.

proposals.title: Therapeutic Reprogramming of the Immunogenomic Landscape in Pulmonary Large Cell Neuroendocrine Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: Pulmonary large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) is an aggressive malignancy with a poor prognosis and limited treatment options. Pre-2026 genomic and transcriptomic analyses, including our own integrated characterization, have consistently classified LCNEC as an immunologically 'cold' tumor with low T-cell infiltration and high myeloid cell signatures, suggesting inherent resistance to immune checkpoint blockade. This fixed immunogenomic profile is a major therapeutic barrier, and the field lacks strategies to dynamically remodel this suppressive microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted pharmacological inhibition of protein arginine methyltransferase (PRMT) activity, known to regulate myeloid cell function and cancer cell expression programs, can remodel the LCNEC tumor immune microenvironment from a 'cold' to a 'hot' state, thereby sensitizing tumors to anti-PD-1 immunotherapy.
proposals.method: We will utilize our established humanized mouse models, engrafted with patient-derived LCNEC cells, to study human immunity in vivo. Mice will be treated with a selective PRMT5 inhibitor (e.g., GSK3326595) alone or in combination with anti-PD-1. We will perform high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and CITE-seq (Cellular Indexing of Transcriptomes and Epitopes by Sequencing) on treated tumors to profile immune and cancer cell expression changes at single-cell resolution. Computational analysis will employ Seurat for clustering, trajectory inference (Monocle3), and cell-cell communication analysis (CellChat) to quantify shifts in myeloid and T-cell populations and activation states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence that the immunogenomic profile of LCNEC is malleable and can be therapeutically targeted. It will identify PRMT5 inhibition as a potential combinatorial strategy to overcome immunotherapy resistance in LCNEC. Furthermore, the detailed single-cell atlas will reveal specific myeloid and T-cell subsets responsible for the 'cold' phenotype and its reversal.
proposals.novelty: While PRMT inhibitors are being explored in cancer, their application to remodel the specific, recalcitrant immune microenvironment of LCNEC is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates Flavell's expertise in humanized mouse models, immunogenomics, and metabolic regulation (e.g., FABP5 in skin immunity) to address a fixed paradigm in neuroendocrine cancer biology.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptional Profiling of Human Skin Explants Reveals Nod1-Dependent Innate Immune Circuits in Antiviral Defense
proposals.motivation: The skin is a critical barrier and immune organ, yet the precise cellular and molecular mechanisms by which resident and recruited immune cells coordinate antiviral responses remain poorly defined at a systems level. Pre-2026, the role of cytosolic innate immune sensors like Nod1 in skin-specific immunity, particularly in human tissue, is underexplored. This gap limits our ability to develop targeted immunomodulatory strategies for cutaneous viral infections and inflammatory skin diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Nod1 activation in human skin explants will induce a distinct, cell-type-specific transcriptional program in keratinocytes and tissue-resident immune cells, which orchestrates a protective antiviral state against a model cutaneous virus.
proposals.method: We will establish ex vivo infections of human skin explants from elective surgeries with a model virus (e.g., HSV-1) in the presence or absence of a Nod1 ligand. Single-cell suspensions will be processed for droplet-based single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics Chromium). Data will be analyzed using the Seurat pipeline (v4) for clustering, cell type annotation, and differential expression. Trajectory analysis will be performed with Monocle3 to infer cellular state transitions. Key findings will be validated by flow cytometry and spatial transcriptomics (Visium) on parallel sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of the human skin immune compartment's response to viral challenge and Nod1 stimulation. It will identify novel cellular subsets and intercellular communication networks critical for cutaneous defense. The data will serve as a foundational resource for understanding immunopathology in diseases like atopic dermatitis, where barrier dysfunction and infection risk intersect.
proposals.novelty: While scRNA-seq has been applied to steady-state and cancerous skin, its use to deconvolute dynamic, receptor-driven antiviral responses in intact human skin tissue is novel. Combining a defined innate immune trigger (Nod1) with viral infection in an explant model bridges molecular immunology with human tissue pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Defining the Plasticity and Functional Overlap of Osx+, Lepr+, and Endothelial Stromal Niches in Skin Homeostasis and Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Stromal cells in the skin, including mesenchymal (e.g., Osx+ osteoblast-lineage, Lepr+ perivascular) and endothelial populations, are critical organizers of immune cell niches and tissue repair. Pre-2026 work has largely studied these lineages in isolation using stringent Cre-driver models, potentially obscuring their plasticity and shared functions. Understanding the overlap and context-dependent reprogramming between these stromal subsets is essential for deciphering their roles in chronic inflammatory skin diseases like atopic dermatitis and cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Osx+, Lepr+, and endothelial stromal cells in the skin exhibit significant transcriptional and functional plasticity, with overlapping support functions for immune cells and tissue repair that become dysregulated in inflammatory disease contexts.
proposals.method: We will employ multi-recombinase fate-mapping mouse models (e.g., Osx-Cre; Lepr-CreER; Cdh5-CreER) with multi-color reporters to track lineage overlap and plasticity during skin homeostasis and imiquimod-induced or MC903-induced atopic dermatitis. Stromal populations will be isolated via FACS and profiled using single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics) combined with cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (CITE-seq) for surface markers. Bioinformatic analysis will use Seurat for clustering, Monocle3 for trajectory inference, and CellChat for intercellular communication modeling to define shared and unique support functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a high-resolution map of stromal cell plasticity in the skin, identifying key transitional states and conserved molecular programs for immune support. It will directly inform how stromal niches are remodeled in inflammation, providing novel stromal-specific targets for therapeutic intervention in skin diseases.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond the canonical, rigid classification of stromal lineages to interrogate their inherent plasticity and functional redundancy in vivo, a concept underexplored in skin biology prior to 2026.

proposals.title: Defining the Lepr+ Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Niche for Natural Killer Cell Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: Natural Killer (NK) cells are critical for anti-tumor and anti-viral immunity, but their survival and maintenance in peripheral tissues like the skin and bone marrow are poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by the Flavell lab and others has highlighted the importance of stromal niches in immune cell regulation, with Lepr+ mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) implicated in hematopoietic support. Identifying the specific molecular signals from Lepr+ MSCs that sustain mature NK cells is essential for understanding tissue immunity and developing therapies for cancer and immune deficiencies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Lepr+ MSCs provide a unique survival niche for mature NK cells through a defined set of contact-dependent (e.g., adhesion molecules) and soluble (e.g., cytokines, metabolites) factors that are distinct from those supporting other lymphocytes.
proposals.method: We will use an ex vivo co-culture system of fluorescence-activated cell sorted (FACS) Lepr+ MSCs from reporter mice with mature NK cells from humanized mice or human donors, mirroring Flavell's expertise in engineering mice to study human immunity. NK cell survival will be quantified by flow cytometry and live-cell imaging. To identify key factors, we will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on co-cultured MSCs and NK cells, followed by targeted CRISPR-Cas9 knockout in MSCs of candidate genes (e.g., Prmt family members implicated in signaling) and blockade of candidate soluble factors (e.g., IL-15, lipids) with neutralizing antibodies or inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular signature of the Lepr+ MSC niche for NK cell survival, filling a major gap in stromal immunology. It will provide a foundational resource for manipulating NK cell numbers in tissues, with direct implications for enhancing NK-based cancer immunotherapies and understanding NK deficiencies in inflammatory skin diseases.
proposals.novelty: While stromal support for B and T cells is studied, the specific niche for mature NK cells is undefined. This proposal uniquely focuses on the Lepr+ MSC subset and employs an integrated, factor-specific screening approach in a human-relevant model system, moving beyond descriptive correlation to mechanistic discovery.

proposals.title: Cell-type-specific dissection of MKP5 reveals hepatocyte-intrinsic regulation of liver inflammation and fibrosis
proposals.motivation: The dual-specificity phosphatase MKP5 (DUSP10) is a critical negative regulator of stress-activated MAP kinases (p38/JNK) in immunity and inflammation. Global MKP5 deficiency in mice leads to severe liver pathology, but the relative contributions of hepatocytes versus hepatic non-parenchymal cells (NPCs) like Kupffer cells (KCs) and hepatic stellate cells (HSCs) are unknown. Resolving this is essential for understanding cell-specific inflammatory signaling in liver diseases, a major pre-2026 clinical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dominant phenotype observed in global MKP5-KO mice is driven by MKP5 loss in hepatocytes, not in Kupffer cells or hepatic stellate cells, and that hepatocyte-intrinsic MKP5 regulates a specific transcriptional program controlling liver inflammation and fibrogenesis.
proposals.method: We will generate novel, inducible, cell-type-specific knockout mice using Cre drivers (Alb-CreERT2 for hepatocytes, Clec4F-CreERT2 for KCs, Lrat-CreERT2 for HSCs) crossed to floxed MKP5 mice. Mice will be challenged with carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) to induce fibrosis or a high-fat diet for steatohepatitis. Phenotypes will be assessed via histology, serum ALT/AST, and quantification of inflammatory and fibrotic markers (e.g., α-SMA, Collagen1a1). Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics platform) of dissociated livers will define cell-type-specific transcriptomic changes and intercellular communication networks (using CellPhoneDB v2.0) in each KO model.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively assign the MKP5-mediated liver phenotype to a specific hepatic cell compartment, fundamentally advancing our understanding of compartmentalized MAP kinase signaling in vivo. It will identify novel hepatocyte-specific transcriptional targets of MKP5-regulated p38/JNK, providing potential therapeutic targets for inflammatory and fibrotic liver diseases.
proposals.novelty: While global MKP5-KO phenotypes are known, this study is the first to dissect its function with precision genetics across the major liver cell types, moving beyond correlative bulk tissue analysis to a causal, mechanistic understanding of cell-intrinsic versus non-cell-autonomous effects.

proposals.title: The Dual-Specificity Phosphatase DUSP10/MKP5 as a Regulator of Metabolic Inflammation in Human NASH Pathogenesis
proposals.motivation: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) is a leading cause of liver disease, driven by chronic inflammation and immune cell activation in the liver. Pre-2026 work has established that MAP kinase pathways (JNK, p38) are central to hepatocyte injury and macrophage-driven inflammation in NASH, but the endogenous negative regulators that modulate these pathways in human disease are poorly defined. Defining these checkpoints could reveal novel therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the MAP kinase phosphatase MKP5 (DUSP10) is a critical negative regulator of hepatic inflammation in NASH, and that its expression or function is dysregulated in human NASH livers, contributing to disease progression.
proposals.method: We will analyze MKP5 expression in a well-characterized cohort of human liver biopsies from healthy controls, NAFLD, and NASH patients using multiplex immunofluorescence (CODEX) and spatial transcriptomics to map MKP5 to specific cell types (hepatocytes, Kupffer cells, infiltrating immune cells). Functional validation will use primary human hepatocytes and monocyte-derived macrophages treated with NASH-relevant insults (palmitate, LPS) in vitro, where we will modulate MKP5 expression via siRNA and CRISPRa to assess impacts on p38/JNK phosphorylation, cytokine production, and lipid accumulation. We will engineer a humanized mouse model (e.g., HIS mice) with hepatocyte- or myeloid-specific MKP5 deletion to study its role in a human immune context during diet-induced NASH.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the cell-specific expression pattern and functional role of MKP5 in human NASH for the first time. It will establish whether MKP5 acts as a protective brake on inflammation and identify if its loss is a feature of advanced disease. The work will bridge fundamental immunology with clinical hepatology, highlighting a specific phosphatase as a potential target for restoring immune homeostasis in the fatty liver.
proposals.novelty: While MKP5's role in T cell biology and autoimmunity is known, its function in parenchymal liver cells and tissue-resident macrophages in human metabolic disease is unexplored. Integrating spatial 'omics in human samples with functional studies in humanized models specifically addresses a gap in translating murine immunology to human NASH pathology.

proposals.title: Defining the S1PR4-Dependent Immunomodulatory Axis: Disentangling GPCR-Specific Signaling from Off-Target Effects in Skin Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor 4 (S1PR4) is a key regulator of immune cell trafficking and function, implicated in inflammatory skin diseases like atopic dermatitis. However, the therapeutic potential of targeting S1PR4 is confounded by the possibility that observed immune effects in vivo may be mediated by off-target interactions of S1P with other receptors or non-GPCR pathways, a critical ambiguity that hinders rational drug design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the immunomodulatory effects attributed to S1P in skin inflammation are primarily mediated through canonical S1PR4-GPCR signaling in specific immune cell subsets, rather than through off-target receptor interactions or non-GPCR mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach using genetically engineered mouse models. First, we will generate and validate conditional S1PR4-knockout mice crossed to cell-specific Cre lines (e.g., Cd4-Cre for T cells, LysM-Cre for myeloid cells). Second, we will use a well-established murine model of atopic dermatitis (e.g., MC903-induced) to compare disease progression, immune cell infiltration (via spectral flow cytometry), and cytokine profiles in global versus cell-specific S1PR4-deficient mice versus wild-type controls. Third, we will perform RNA-sequencing on sorted immune cell populations from inflamed skin to define the S1PR4-dependent transcriptional program and validate key pathways using phospho-flow cytometry for downstream GPCR effectors (e.g., pERK, pAkt).
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish S1PR4 as the primary relevant host target for S1P-mediated effects in a model of skin inflammation, delineating the specific immune cell compartments involved. It will provide a clear molecular and cellular map of S1PR4 signaling in vivo, offering validated targets for future selective therapeutic intervention in inflammatory skin diseases.
proposals.novelty: While S1PR4's role is recognized, this study uniquely isolates its GPCR-specific function from other S1P-mediated mechanisms by integrating cell-type-specific genetic deletion with high-dimensional immune phenotyping in a relevant disease model, moving beyond pharmacological inhibition which lacks receptor specificity.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Engineered Human Cytokine and Niche Factor Cocktails for Enhanced Immune Cell Maturation in Humanized Mice
proposals.motivation: Current humanized mouse models, while transformative for studying human immunity, often exhibit suboptimal or aberrant immune cell development and function, limiting their fidelity to the human system. This deficiency is partly attributed to the reliance on a limited set of known human cytokines (e.g., SCF, IL-3, GM-CSF) that may not fully recapitulate the complex niche required for robust human hematopoiesis and immune maturation. Identifying novel or engineered factors is crucial for improving these models to better study human diseases, including cancer and inflammatory skin conditions like atopic dermatitis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the current cytokine milieu supporting human immune cells in vivo is insufficient, and that systematic screening for novel or engineered human cytokine/niche factor combinations will yield optimized cocktails that significantly enhance the maturation, function, and tissue-specific residency of human immune cells in humanized mice.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput in vivo screening platform using NSG-SGM3 mice engrafted with human hematopoietic stem cells. A library of candidate factors, including understudied cytokines, metabolic regulators (informed by FABP5/ether lipid insights), and engineered variants with enhanced stability/receptor affinity, will be delivered via hydrodynamic gene transfer or sustained-release matrices. Single-cell RNA-sequencing and mass cytometry (CyTOF) of bone marrow, spleen, and skin will be used to comprehensively profile immune cell populations, comparing their transcriptional states, surface protein expression, and functional capacity (e.g., cytokine production, cytotoxicity) to cells from standard models and human reference data.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a new, optimized cytokine support system for humanized mouse models, directly enhancing their utility for preclinical immunotherapy and disease modeling. It will identify specific factor combinations that promote the development of under-represented immune subsets, such as tissue-resident memory T cells or specific innate lymphoid cells, relevant to skin and cancer immunology. The findings will provide a foundational resource for engineering more human-relevant immune niches in vivo.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond supplementing known factors, this proposal systematically screens for novel and engineered factors in an in vivo context, integrating pre-2026 humanized mouse engineering with single-cell multi-omics to deconvolve their effects on specific human immune lineages. It uniquely links cytokine discovery to tissue-specific immune maturation, particularly in the skin, a key Flavell lab interest.

proposals.title: Engineering a Long-Lived Humanized Mouse Model to Decipher Immune Aging and Chronic Inflammation
proposals.motivation: The study of chronic human immune processes, such as age-related inflammation (inflammaging), autoimmunity, and cancer immunoediting, is severely limited by the short (~2-year) lifespan of standard laboratory mice. This precludes longitudinal studies of slow-progressing human diseases within a single model organism. Existing humanized mouse models, while transformative for acute studies, succumb to age-related pathologies before chronic immune phenotypes fully manifest, creating a critical gap in our ability to model human immune aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering a progeria-resistant, lifespan-extended humanized mouse host by targeting conserved aging pathways will enable the faithful recapitulation and mechanistic dissection of chronic human immune processes that unfold over a longer timescale.
proposals.method: We will utilize CRISPR/Cas9 to introduce known longevity-associated alleles (e.g., the *Pcsk9* knockout or *BubR1* hypomorphic mutations) into the immunodeficient *NSG* (NOD-scid IL2Rγnull) background. These engineered hosts will then be humanized via engraftment of human hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) to create the 'LL-HuNSG' model. We will rigorously characterize immune reconstitution, function, and tissue residency over an extended 3-year period, comparing it to standard humanized controls. Chronic processes will be studied by introducing human immune cells from patients with atopic dermatitis or from aged donors, tracking disease progression and immune exhaustion markers longitudinally.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first durable platform for studying human immune aging and the lifelong dynamics of human immune cells in vivo. It will directly enable experiments on the role of chronic, low-grade inflammation in age-related diseases and cancer, and allow for the testing of long-term immunotherapies. The model will bridge a major technical gap between mouse lifespan and human disease timelines.
proposals.novelty: While humanized mice and longevity models exist separately, their integration to specifically overcome the temporal limitation in immunology is novel. This approach moves beyond using progeria models to study accelerated aging, instead creating a stabilized host to study normal-pace human immune aging, which is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Myeloid-Derived IL-10 as a Dominant Immunosuppressive Mediator in Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma Progression and Metastasis
proposals.motivation: IL-10 is a potent immunosuppressive cytokine canonically linked to regulatory T cell (Treg) function in cancer. However, pre-2026 studies, including those from the Flavell lab on engineering mice to study human immunity, highlight the complexity of the tumor microenvironment where myeloid cells are abundant and functionally plastic. The specific cellular source of IL-10 that dominantly suppresses anti-tumor immunity in different cancer contexts, particularly in epithelial cancers like cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), remains unclear, limiting targeted therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in cSCC and its metastatic niches, IL-10 derived from tumor-associated myeloid cells, rather than Tregs, is the dominant source mediating local immune suppression and facilitating tumor progression.
proposals.method: We will employ cell-type-specific IL-10 deletion using established pre-2026 mouse models (e.g., LysM-Cre x Il10fl/fl for myeloid cells and Foxp3-Cre x Il10fl/fl for Tregs) in a chemically-induced and genetically-engineered (e.g., K-Ras/p53-driven) model of metastatic cSCC. Tumor growth and metastasis will be monitored. We will perform high-dimensional flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on dissociated tumors and metastatic lymph nodes to comprehensively map immune cell populations, their activation states, and IL-10 expression. Spatial transcriptomics (Visium) will be used to correlate myeloid-derived IL-10 expression with immunosuppressive niches and metastatic foci.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively identify the dominant cellular source of immunosuppressive IL-10 in a relevant skin cancer model, challenging the Treg-centric paradigm. It will provide a high-resolution map of the immunosuppressive myeloid landscape in cSCC progression and metastasis, identifying new cellular targets for combination immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While myeloid cells are known to produce IL-10, directly testing and proving their dominance over Treg-derived IL-10 in suppressing anti-tumor immunity within a specific cancer type and its metastatic niche is a novel inversion of the established source-target relationship.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Validation of IL-10 Autocrine Amplification Loops in Non-Treg Immune Cell Populations within the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from the Flavell lab, has established that an IL-10 autocrine loop in regulatory T cells (Tregs) is critical for their stability and suppressive function in tumors, representing a key immunosuppressive mechanism. However, the tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem of diverse immune cell types, many of which can produce and respond to IL-10. It remains unknown whether this potent self-reinforcing regulatory circuit is a Treg-specific feature or a more general immune-modulatory program co-opted by other cell states to influence tumor immunity and progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that analogous, functionally significant IL-10 autocrine amplification loops exist in other tumor-infiltrating immune cell populations, such as myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), specific macrophage subsets, or CD8+ T cell exhaustion precursors, and that these loops contribute uniquely to the immunosuppressive landscape.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established 'Engineering Mice to Study Human Immunity' platform, using humanized mouse models engrafted with patient-derived tumor organoids to recapitulate the human TME. We will perform high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and CITE-seq on sorted tumor-infiltrating leukocytes to identify non-Treg populations co-expressing IL10 and its high-affinity receptor (IL10RA/IL10RB). Candidate populations will be isolated and their autocrine function tested using CRISPR-Cas9-mediated knockout of IL10 or IL10R in vitro, assessing STAT3 phosphorylation and downstream gene expression. The in vivo role will be validated by adoptive transfer of gene-edited cells into tumor-bearing mice, followed by multiplexed imaging and flow cytometry to assess tumor growth and immune cell states.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map IL-10 autocrine signaling beyond Tregs, potentially revealing new cellular targets for cancer immunotherapy. It will advance our fundamental understanding of how pleiotropic cytokines like IL-10 can enact cell-type-specific regulatory programs to shape the overall immune response to cancer.
proposals.novelty: While IL-10's role in the TME is recognized, the search for and functional validation of active autocrine signaling loops in non-Treg populations is unexplored. This shifts the focus from a Treg-centric view to a circuit-based understanding of immunosuppression across the entire TME cellular network.

proposals.title: Mechanistic dissection of IL-10-induced PD-L1 upregulation in human monocytes via STAT3-dependent and -independent pathways
proposals.motivation: IL-10 is a critical anti-inflammatory cytokine that suppresses immune responses, in part by upregulating PD-L1 on monocytes, a key immune checkpoint. However, the precise intracellular signaling cascade linking the IL-10 receptor to PD-L1 gene transcription remains incompletely mapped, particularly in human cells. Defining this pathway is crucial for understanding immune homeostasis and for developing targeted therapies in cancer and autoimmune diseases where IL-10 signaling is dysregulated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IL-10 upregulates PD-L1 on human monocytes primarily through JAK1/STAT3 signaling, but that a parallel, STAT3-independent pathway involving PRMT-mediated arginine methylation contributes to the full transcriptional response.
proposals.method: We will use primary human monocytes isolated from healthy donors and engineered mice with humanized immune systems. Following IL-10 stimulation, we will perform phosphoproteomics and RNA-seq to identify activated kinases and immediate-early genes. STAT3 dependency will be tested using CRISPR-Cas9 knockout and a specific STAT3 inhibitor (Stattic). Co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectrometry will identify PRMTs (e.g., PRMT1, PRMT5) recruited to the IL-10 receptor complex, with functional validation using PRMT inhibitors (e.g., GSK591). PD-L1 surface expression will be quantified by flow cytometry, and promoter binding assessed by ChIP-seq for STAT3 and histone methylation marks (H4R3me2s).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive molecular map of IL-10 signaling to PD-L1 in human monocytes, identifying novel kinase substrates and epigenetic regulators. It will establish whether STAT3 is the sole mediator or part of a redundant network, offering new potential targets (e.g., specific PRMTs) for modulating the PD-1/PD-L1 axis in immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While STAT3's role is implied, a systematic proteomic and genetic dissection in primary human monocytes is lacking. The investigation of PRMTs as potential IL-10 signal transducers, linking cytokine signaling to epigenetic regulation of PD-L1, is a novel and non-obvious angle based on Flavell lab's expertise in immunometabolism and PRMT biology.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Chromatin Landscape of IL-10-Mediated Immunoregulation in Human Monocytes and Tregs
proposals.motivation: IL-10 is a critical anti-inflammatory cytokine essential for resolving immune responses and preventing autoimmunity, yet its cell-type-specific epigenetic mechanisms in human immune cells remain poorly resolved. Understanding how IL-10 reprograms chromatin accessibility in key regulatory cells like monocytes and regulatory T cells (Tregs) is vital for deciphering its role in inflammatory diseases and cancer, areas of active investigation in the Flavell lab. This proposal directly bridges the lab's expertise in human immunology, disease models, and epigenetic regulation (e.g., PRMTs) to a high-resolution, mechanistic question.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IL-10 induces distinct, cell-type-specific changes in chromatin accessibility in human monocytes and Tregs, revealing novel cis-regulatory elements and transcription factor networks that drive its immunosuppressive program.
proposals.method: We will isolate CD14+ monocytes and CD4+CD25+CD127lo Tregs from healthy human donor PBMCs, treat them with recombinant human IL-10, and perform single-cell ATAC-seq using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Data will be processed with Cell Ranger ATAC and analyzed using ArchR and Signac for peak calling, dimensionality reduction, and identification of differentially accessible regions. Transcription factor motif enrichment analysis will be performed with chromVAR and HOMER. Validation will utilize CUT&RUN for histone marks and qPCR of putative target genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of IL-10-driven epigenetic remodeling in primary human immune cells, identifying novel regulatory elements and candidate master regulators. It will provide a foundational resource for understanding immune tolerance mechanisms and inform therapeutic strategies for IL-10-related pathologies, including inflammatory disorders and cancer immunotherapy resistance.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies exist, applying single-cell epigenomics to dissect IL-10's effects simultaneously in monocytes and Tregs will reveal heterogeneous cellular responses and co-regulated pathways not observable in population averages, integrating directly with the lab's focus on engineering humanized mouse models for functional validation.

proposals.title: Gut Microbiome-Derived Metabolites Modulate Regulatory T Cell IL-10 Production to Govern Liver Metastatic Niche Formation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work establishes that the gut microbiome influences systemic immunity and cancer progression, and that regulatory T cell (Treg)-derived IL-10 is a critical immunosuppressive cytokine. Liver metastasis is a major cause of mortality in cancers like colorectal carcinoma, yet the mechanistic link connecting specific microbial metabolites to hepatic Treg function and metastatic susceptibility remains poorly defined. This gap limits the development of microbiome-targeted interventions for metastasis prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific gut microbiome-derived metabolites, particularly short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and secondary bile acids, program intestinal Tregs for enhanced IL-10 production, which systemically suppresses anti-tumor immunity in the liver to foster a permissive niche for metastatic colonization.
proposals.method: We will use gnotobiotic mice, colonized with defined microbial consortia or human patient-derived microbiota, coupled with established liver metastasis models (e.g., intrasplenic injection of MC38 or CT26 cells). Treg-specific IL-10 knockout mice (Foxp3-Cre x Il10fl/fl) will determine the necessity of Treg-derived IL-10. We will employ multi-parameter flow cytometry, single-cell RNA-Seq of hepatic immune cells, and targeted metabolomics of portal venous blood to correlate microbial metabolites with Treg phenotype and function. PRMT5 inhibition, a known modulator of Treg stability, will be tested as a potential intervention to disrupt this axis.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate a precise microbiome → metabolite → Treg (IL-10) → liver metastasis axis, moving beyond correlation to mechanism. It will identify candidate microbial metabolites and hepatic immune pathways that are actionable therapeutic targets. The findings will directly extend Flavell's work on engineering mice to study human immunity by providing a framework for translating microbiome-immune discoveries from murine models to human metastatic disease.
proposals.novelty: While microbiome effects on cancer are known, linking specific microbial metabolites to the functional programming of Treg IL-10 production specifically for liver metastasis is novel. Integrating metabolomic profiling of portal circulation with single-cell analysis of the hepatic immune niche to deconstruct this axis represents a significant technical and conceptual advance over existing associative studies.

proposals.title: Patient-Specific MCJ Expression as a Determinant of CAR-T Cell Metabolic Fitness and Therapeutic Durability
proposals.motivation: Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy achieves remarkable responses in hematologic cancers, but durability is limited by T-cell exhaustion, driven by metabolic dysfunction. Pre-2026 work identifies MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein) as a mitochondrial brake whose loss in T cells enhances oxidative metabolism and persistence in chronic infection and tumor models. However, it is unknown if natural variation in MCJ expression among human patients predicts the metabolic fitness of their manufactured CAR-T products, representing a critical gap for personalized immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that baseline MCJ expression levels in a patient's native T cells dictate the metabolic resilience of the resulting CAR-T cells upon chronic antigen exposure, with low MCJ conferring superior mitochondrial fitness and durability.
proposals.method: We will isolate T cells from healthy donors and patients with B-cell malignancies (pre-CAR-T infusion). Using flow cytometry and qPCR, we will stratify donors into MCJ-high and MCJ-low cohorts. T cells from each cohort will be engineered with a CD19-targeting CAR and subjected to repetitive in vitro stimulation with antigen-expressing target cells. We will perform longitudinal metabolic profiling using Seahorse analysis to measure oxidative phosphorylation and glycolytic capacity, quantify mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (ROS) by MitoSOX staining, and assess exhaustion markers (PD-1, TIM-3) and proliferative capacity. Statistical correlation between baseline MCJ, metabolic parameters, and functional readouts will be performed.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a direct, patient-specific mechanistic link between MCJ expression and CAR-T cell metabolic fitness, moving beyond murine models to human immunology. It will provide a potential predictive biomarker (MCJ expression) to identify patients at risk for suboptimal CAR-T persistence. The findings will lay the groundwork for precision adjunct therapies, such as antioxidants or metabolic modulators, to rescue the function of metabolically compromised CAR-T products.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in T-cell metabolism is known, applying this as a patient-stratifying biomarker for CAR-T cell manufacturing outcome is novel. The study uniquely focuses on intrinsic human T-cell heterogeneity as a deterministic factor in engineered cell product function, bridging basic immunometabolism and clinical translation.

proposals.title: Chronic Mitochondrial Coiled-Coil Protein J (MCJ) Inhibition Impairs T Cell Fitness by Inducing Metabolic Stress and Pathological ROS Production
proposals.motivation: MCJ is a mitochondrial inner membrane protein that negatively regulates Complex I, and its inhibition has been proposed as a strategy to enhance T cell metabolism and anti-tumor function. However, the long-term consequences of MCJ silencing on T cell metabolic homeostasis and redox balance are unknown. Given the critical role of balanced metabolism for sustained immune responses in cancer and chronic inflammatory diseases, understanding potential adverse effects is crucial for translational applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, rather than acute, genetic or pharmacological inhibition of MCJ in T cells will lead to a harmful metabolic imbalance, characterized by excessive mitochondrial respiration, depletion of key metabolic intermediates, and pathological overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS), ultimately impairing T cell survival and function.
proposals.method: We will generate T cell-specific, inducible MCJ knockout mice (using Cre-loxP systems) to model chronic silencing. Isolated T cells (naïve, activated, and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes) will be analyzed using Seahorse metabolic flux assays to measure oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis. We will quantify mitochondrial ROS (MitoSOX) and total cellular ROS, and perform targeted metabolomics (LC-MS) to assess TCA cycle intermediates and antioxidant pools (e.g., GSH). Functional outcomes will be assessed in vivo using established melanoma (B16) and autoimmune dermatitis models, correlating metabolic profiles with T cell persistence, exhaustion markers (e.g., PD-1, TIM-3), and disease outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the safety limits of MCJ-targeted immunometabolic therapies by revealing a potential 'tipping point' where beneficial metabolic enhancement becomes detrimental stress. It will provide a comprehensive dataset on the longitudinal metabolic and redox adaptations of T cells to chronic MCJ loss. The findings will directly inform the development of combination strategies, such as with antioxidants, for future therapeutic interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on the acute benefits of MCJ inhibition for T cell activation, this proposal uniquely investigates the chronic, pathological consequences, integrating metabolic flux, redox biology, and functional immunity in vivo. The use of inducible, T cell-specific knockout models to separate acute from chronic effects is a novel approach not previously applied to MCJ biology.

proposals.title: MCJ is a lineage-defining metabolic checkpoint for human CD4+ T cell subset differentiation and function
proposals.motivation: Helper T cell subsets (Th1, Th2, Th17, Treg) require distinct metabolic programs to support their differentiation and functions, which are critical for effective immunity and implicated in autoimmune and allergic diseases. The mitochondrial protein MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein) is a negative regulator of mitochondrial respiration, and pre-2026 studies in mice suggest it influences T cell memory and exhaustion, but its causal, mechanistic role in human CD4+ subset specification remains unknown. Determining if MCJ acts as a metabolic switch guiding human T cell fate would reveal a new target for modulating immune responses in diseases like atopic dermatitis or cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MCJ is a critical metabolic determinant whose expression is differentially regulated during polarization, and that its genetic deletion will disrupt subset-specific metabolic reprogramming, thereby altering the differentiation, cytokine profile, and functional capacity of specific human CD4+ T helper and regulatory T cell lineages.
proposals.method: Primary human naive CD4+ T cells will be isolated from healthy donor blood. MCJ will be deleted using CRISPR-Cas9 ribonucleoprotein electroporation prior to in vitro polarization under defined conditions for Th1, Th2, Th17, and iTreg subsets. Metabolic analysis will employ Agilent Seahorse XF Analyzers to measure real-time oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis, complemented by targeted LC-MS metabolomics (e.g., measuring TCA cycle intermediates, amino acids). Functional readouts will include multiplex cytokine ELISAs, flow cytometry for lineage-specific transcription factors and surface markers, and in vitro suppression assays for Tregs. Data integration will use established bioinformatics tools (e.g., MetaboAnalyst 5.0, GraphPad Prism) for statistical analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive, causal link between MCJ and the metabolic programming of human T cell subsets, moving beyond correlative murine studies. It will identify MCJ as a potential therapeutic node for selectively modulating pathogenic (e.g., Th2 in allergy) or suppressive (Treg) T cell responses in human disease.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic control of T cell fate is recognized, the specific role of MCJ as a mitochondrial brake has not been mechanistically dissected in primary human CD4+ subsets. This study uniquely combines precise genetic perturbation in human cells with integrated metabolic and functional profiling to test if MCJ is a lineage-specific checkpoint, not just a general modulator.

proposals.title: Targeted Nanoparticle Delivery for Transient MCJ Inhibition Reveals Subset-Specific Metabolic Control of Human T Cell Function
proposals.motivation: The mitochondrial protein MCJ (DnaJC15) is a negative regulator of mitochondrial respiration, and its inhibition can enhance T cell metabolic fitness and anti-tumor activity. However, T cell subsets (e.g., effector, regulatory, memory) have distinct metabolic requirements for their specialized functions. A lack of tools for temporally and selectively modulating metabolism in specific human T cell populations in vivo has hindered our understanding of MCJ's differential roles and its therapeutic potential in cancer and autoimmune diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transient, subset-specific inhibition of MCJ using ligand-targeted nanoparticles will differentially reprogram the metabolism and function of distinct human T cell subsets, creating unique and reversible therapeutic windows to enhance anti-tumor immunity or suppress autoimmunity.
proposals.method: We will engineer poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles loaded with MCJ-targeting siRNA and functionalized with surface-conjugated antibodies or aptamers against subset-specific surface markers (e.g., CD4, CD25, CD45RO). We will validate selective uptake and transient MCJ knockdown in sorted human T cell subsets in vitro, assessing metabolic flux (Seahorse), proliferation, and cytokine profiles. Subsequently, we will employ a humanized mouse model (e.g., NSG mice reconstituted with a human immune system) to test in vivo delivery and the functional impact of transient MCJ inhibition in specific subsets during tumor challenge or in an autoimmune model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for the precise, transient metabolic manipulation of defined immune cell populations. It will provide the first direct comparison of how MCJ inhibition distinctly affects human T cell subsets, defining subset-specific metabolic vulnerabilities. The findings will guide the development of more precise immunometabolic therapies for cancer and autoimmune disorders.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in T cells is known, combining biodegradable, ligand-targeted nanoparticles for transient RNAi delivery with a focus on comparing human T cell subsets in vivo represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance over bulk or permanent genetic approaches.

proposals.title: Metabolic Reprogramming via MCJ Silencing Enhances CAR T-Cell Persistence and Anti-Tumor Function in Humanized Mouse Models
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of CAR T-cell therapy for solid tumors is T-cell exhaustion and poor persistence, linked to metabolic insufficiency. Pre-2026 work identifies mitochondrial dysfunction as a key driver of T-cell exhaustion. MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) is a mitochondrial inner membrane protein that negatively regulates Complex I; its genetic or epigenetic silencing in mice enhances CD8+ T-cell memory and anti-tumor responses, but its role in human CAR T-cells is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Silencing MCJ in human CAR T-cells will reprogram their mitochondrial metabolism, leading to enhanced oxidative phosphorylation, reduced exhaustion, and superior persistence and anti-tumor efficacy in humanized mouse models of solid cancer.
proposals.method: We will engineer anti-mesothelin CAR T-cells (using a 2nd-generation CD28/CD3ζ architecture) with and without MCJ silencing via shRNA from the same lentiviral vector. Cells will be validated in vitro via Seahorse assays (glycolysis, OXPHOS) and mitochondrial imaging. We will employ NSG mice engrafted with human immune cells (CD34+ HSPCs) and orthotopic human mesothelioma tumors. In vivo efficacy, persistence, and exhaustion (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3) will be tracked. Integrated single-cell RNA-seq (10x Genomics) and ATAC-seq on recovered T-cells will define transcriptional and epigenetic states, complemented by LC-MS metabolomic profiling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first mechanistic, multi-omics dataset on MCJ's role in human CAR T-cell biology, directly linking a specific metabolic target to functional outcomes. It will establish preclinical proof-of-concept and identify predictive biomarkers (e.g., metabolic gene signatures, chromatin accessibility) for MCJ-silenced CAR T-cell fitness to inform clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in mouse T-cells is known, its application to human CAR T-cell engineering is novel. The integrated use of humanized models with matched multi-omics profiling to deconstruct the mechanism prior to clinical development represents a rigorous, data-driven translational approach distinct from empirical CAR construct testing.

proposals.title: A Composite T-cell Fitness Index Integrating MCJ Heterogeneity Predicts CAR-T Therapy Efficacy and Relapse
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of CAR-T therapy in hematologic cancers, a significant proportion of patients experience primary resistance or relapse. Pre-2026 work identifies mitochondrial dysfunction, including via the mitochondrial protein MCJ, as a key determinant of T-cell exhaustion and poor persistence. However, clinical translation requires moving beyond single biomarkers to robust, patient-specific predictors that capture the functional heterogeneity of the infused T-cell product.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multivariate composite score, integrating MCJ expression with complementary metrics of mitochondrial and cellular fitness, will more accurately predict CAR-T therapy outcomes and relapse risk than MCJ expression alone.
proposals.method: We will engineer humanized mice (NSG-SGM3) to study human CAR-T cells, as per Flavell's recent methodology. Pre-infusion CAR-T products from B-cell malignancy patients will be profiled via flow cytometry and single-cell RNA-seq for MCJ expression, mitochondrial membrane potential, ROS levels, and glycolytic capacity. We will adapt the ecological Shannon Diversity Index framework to compute a patient-specific 'T-cell Fitness Index' (TFI). This TFI will be correlated with in vivo CAR-T expansion, persistence, and tumor clearance in mice, and validated against clinical outcomes in a retrospective patient cohort using multivariate Cox regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically actionable biomarker for CAR-T patient stratification. It will mechanistically link T-cell product heterogeneity to therapeutic efficacy, providing a framework for improving manufacturing. The study will also validate the use of ecological indices for quantifying cellular system resilience in an immunological context.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of composite biodiversity indices from ecology to quantify T-cell product fitness. It moves beyond correlating single parameters to defining an integrative, systems-level metric for a pressing clinical problem in immunotherapy.

proposals.title: Mitochondrial Respiration Protein MCJ as a Predictive Biomarker for CAR-T Cell Efficacy and Durability in B-Cell Malignancies
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cell therapies for B-cell cancers, a significant proportion of patients experience primary non-response or relapse, often linked to poor CAR-T cell persistence and exhaustion. Pre-2026 research has identified MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein) as a key endogenous inhibitor of mitochondrial respiration in T cells, with its genetic deletion enhancing memory formation and anti-tumor function in murine models. However, the heterogeneity of MCJ expression in human patients and its correlation with clinical outcomes following CAR-T therapy remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low baseline MCJ expression in patient-derived T cells prior to CAR-T manufacturing is associated with superior CAR-T cell metabolic fitness in vivo, leading to improved clinical response and reduced relapse rates.
proposals.method: We will leverage a cohort of B-cell malignancy patients undergoing CD19-targeted CAR-T therapy. Using pre-infusion leukapheresis samples, we will quantify MCJ expression at the single-cell level via flow cytometry (intracellular staining) and RNA-sequencing of isolated T cells. These molecular profiles will be correlated with detailed clinical outcomes, including objective response, progression-free survival, and CAR-T cell expansion/persistence measured by qPCR. Statistical analysis will employ Cox proportional hazards models for survival data and multivariate regression to control for known confounders (e.g., tumor burden, prior lines of therapy). Validation will involve in vitro assays of mitochondrial function (Seahorse) on T-cell subsets stratified by MCJ expression.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish MCJ as a novel, functionally relevant predictive biomarker for CAR-T therapy, potentially enabling patient stratification. It will provide direct human evidence linking intrinsic T-cell metabolic state to therapeutic efficacy, bridging murine immunometabolism studies and clinical oncology. The findings could inform strategies to modulate MCJ for improving next-generation cell therapies.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in murine T-cell memory is known, its translational potential as a pre-manufacturing biomarker in human patients is entirely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates deep molecular profiling of a specific metabolic regulator with longitudinal clinical data from a standardized cellular therapy.

proposals.title: Patient-Specific Immunometabolic Profiling Identifies Synergistic MCJ Silencing Combinations to Overcome T Cell Dysfunction in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Immunotherapies like CAR-T cells often fail in solid tumors due to a metabolically suppressive tumor microenvironment (TME) that induces T cell exhaustion. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, has shown that silencing the mitochondrial protein MCJ can enhance T cell metabolic fitness and anti-tumor function. However, the optimal, patient-specific combination of MCJ silencing with other metabolic modulators to reprogram the TME remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patient-derived tumor-immune co-cultures will reveal distinct immunometabolic subtypes, and that MCJ silencing will synergize with specific, subtype-dependent metabolic interventions to restore anti-tumor immunity.
proposals.method: We will establish a library of patient-derived organoids (PDOs) from solid tumors (e.g., lung cancer) and co-culture them with autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or engineered CAR-T cells. Using mass spectrometry-based metabolomics and Seahorse analysis, we will first profile the baseline immunometabolic state of each pair. We will then perform combinatorial screening, testing MCJ knockdown (via shRNA) alongside a panel of metabolic modulators (e.g., etomoxir, 2-DG, metformin) targeting glycolysis, fatty acid oxidation, and mitochondrial function. Efficacy will be measured by cytokine production, tumor killing, and T cell proliferation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a functional map linking tumor metabolic profiles to effective MCJ-based combination therapies. It will identify predictive biomarkers for patient stratification and deliver a precision medicine framework for immunometabolic intervention, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches to overcome TME-mediated resistance.
proposals.novelty: This study integrates patient-specific models with combinatorial metabolic screening, shifting from generic synergy discovery to identifying predictive, tumor-intrinsic metabolic signatures that dictate response to MCJ-targeted therapy.

proposals.title: Synergistic Metabolic Reprogramming to Enhance CAR-T Cell Persistence and Anti-Tumor Efficacy
proposals.motivation: CAR-T cell therapy shows limited efficacy in solid tumors, partly due to T cell exhaustion driven by the suppressive tumor microenvironment and metabolic insufficiency. Pre-2026 work, including from the Flavell lab, highlights mitochondrial function and immunometabolism as key determinants of T cell fate and function. Silencing MCJ, a mitochondrial regulator, improves T cell metabolism and anti-tumor activity, but combinatorial approaches may be required for maximal effect.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining MCJ silencing with pharmacological modulators of complementary metabolic pathways will synergistically enhance CAR-T cell mitochondrial fitness, persistence, and anti-tumor function against solid tumors.
proposals.method: We will engineer human CAR-T cells targeting a solid tumor antigen (e.g., mesothelin) with shRNA-mediated MCJ knockdown. These cells will be treated in vitro with specific metabolic drugs (e.g., the AMPK activator metformin, the complex I inhibitor metformin, or the FAO inhibitor etomoxir) and assessed for mitochondrial respiration (Seahorse), proliferation, and cytokine production. The optimal combination will be tested in vivo using established humanized mouse models (NSG mice engrafted with human tumor cells), monitoring CAR-T cell persistence, tumor control, and exhaustion markers via flow cytometry and single-cell RNA-seq.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, synergistic metabolic intervention strategy to overcome a major barrier in CAR-T therapy for solid cancers. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how coordinated metabolic rewiring impacts T cell longevity and function, directly informing future clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role is established, its combination with systemic metabolic drugs in CAR-T cells is unexplored. This approach moves beyond single-target modulation to engineer a holistic metabolic state resistant to tumor-induced exhaustion.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Ecological Monitoring of MCJ-Deficient CAR-T Cell Populations Reveals Determinants of In Vivo Persistence and Safety
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of CAR-T cell therapy is limited by T cell exhaustion and poor long-term persistence. MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) is a mitochondrial brake whose silencing enhances T cell metabolism and anti-tumor efficacy in mice, but its long-term effects on CAR-T cell population dynamics and host safety in vivo are unknown. A comprehensive, multi-modal assessment strategy is critically needed to predict and ensure the durable safety and efficacy of such engineered cell therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adopting an ecological monitoring framework will reveal that MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells exhibit superior long-term clonal persistence and delayed exhaustion, but may also induce distinct, measurable shifts in the host immune environment that correlate with long-term safety.
proposals.method: We will engineer human MCJ-silenced CD19-targeting CAR-T cells with a stable, high-diversity lentiviral barcode library for clonal tracking. These cells will be adoptively transferred into immunodeficient NSG mice engrafted with a human hematopoietic system and a B-cell malignancy. We will perform serial, minimally invasive sampling of blood and bone marrow over 6+ months. Samples will be analyzed by high-throughput barcode sequencing for population dynamics, single-cell RNA/TCR-seq for exhaustion states, and mass cytometry for systemic immune profiling. End-organ health will be assessed via histopathology and serum cytokine analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, integrated framework for the longitudinal safety and efficacy assessment of engineered cell therapies. It will provide the first detailed in vivo fate map of MCJ-modulated CAR-T cells, identifying clonal and metabolic correlates of persistence. The findings will generate critical pre-clinical data to guide the translation of metabolic enhancement strategies for CAR-T cells.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and methodological translation of ecological population monitoring (e.g., mark-recapture, environmental impact assessment) to cellular immunotherapy, moving beyond snapshot analyses to a dynamic, systems-level understanding of engineered cell-host interactions over time.

proposals.title: Mapping Tumor Microenvironment Determinants of CAR-T Cell Exhaustion: A Comparative Study of MCJ-Deficient T Cell Persistence Across Solid Cancers
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of CAR-T cell therapy in solid tumors is severely limited by T cell exhaustion within the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Pre-2026 work has identified MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) as a key mitochondrial brake whose silencing enhances T cell metabolism and anti-tumor function in acute models. However, the long-term durability and exhaustion trajectories of MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells across diverse solid tumor types, each with a unique TME, remain unknown, hindering rational clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the long-term persistence and functional durability of MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells will vary significantly across solid tumor types, and that this variation will correlate with specific, measurable TME factors, such as metabolic competition and immunosuppressive ligand expression.
proposals.method: We will engineer human MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells targeting a pan-carcinoma antigen (e.g., mesothelin) using CRISPR-Cas9. These cells will be adoptively transferred into immunodeficient NSG mice bearing patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), and glioblastoma (GBM). Long-term in vivo persistence will be tracked via bioluminescence imaging and flow cytometry of serial blood draws. Exhaustion trajectories will be assessed by longitudinal transcriptional profiling (single-cell RNA-seq) and surface protein expression (e.g., PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3). Tumor-intrinsic factors (metabolite levels, hypoxia, immunosuppressive ligand expression) will be quantified via mass spectrometry and multiplex IHC, and correlated with CAR-T functional decline.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first comparative map linking specific TME features to the rate of functional exhaustion in metabolically enhanced CAR-T cells. It will identify which solid tumor types are most susceptible to MCJ-targeting therapy and pinpoint dominant exhaustion pathways (e.g., metabolic stress vs. chronic checkpoint signaling) in each context. This will directly inform the design of rational, tumor-type-specific combination therapies, such as pairing MCJ silencing with metabolic support agents or specific checkpoint blockade.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies show MCJ silencing boosts acute CAR-T function, this is the first systematic, longitudinal comparison of its impact across multiple, clinically relevant solid tumor microenvironments to define context-specific durability and exhaustion mechanisms, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach.

proposals.title: Targeting Mitochondrial Respiration via MCJ Silencing to Enhance CAR-T Cell Persistence and Anti-Tumor Efficacy in Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: A major limitation of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy, particularly for solid tumors, is T-cell exhaustion and poor long-term persistence, which is linked to metabolic insufficiency in the tumor microenvironment.
