Pre-2026 work has identified MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) as a key mitochondrial brake; its deficiency enhances oxidative phosphorylation and improves T-cell memory and function in acute infection models. This proposal aims to translate this metabolic insight into improving CAR-T cell durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that silencing MCJ in human CAR-T cells will reprogram their mitochondrial metabolism, leading to enhanced long-term persistence, reduced exhaustion, and superior anti-tumor efficacy in solid tumor models without inducing significant toxicity.
proposals.method: We will engineer MCJ-deficient human CAR-T cells targeting a solid tumor antigen (e.g., mesothelin) using CRISPR-Cas9 or shRNA. These cells will be infused into immunodeficient NSG mice bearing established patient-derived xenograft (PDX) tumors. Long-term in vivo persistence will be monitored via bioluminescence imaging and flow cytometry of serial blood draws. Exhaustion markers (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3), metabolic profiling (Seahorse analyzer), and multiplex cytokine analysis will be performed on recovered T cells. Potential toxicity, including cytokine release syndrome and on-target/off-tumor effects, will be assessed via serum cytokines and histopathology of major organs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive longitudinal dataset on the durability and safety of metabolically enhanced CAR-T cells via MCJ silencing. It will establish a direct causal link between a specific mitochondrial regulator and CAR-T cell fate in vivo, offering a novel, clinically translatable strategy to overcome a core barrier in adoptive cell therapy for solid cancers.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in T-cell memory is established in infection models, its application to genetically engineered therapeutic T cells for long-term cancer control is unexplored. This study uniquely integrates a precise metabolic target with human CAR-T cell engineering and rigorous long-term in vivo modeling of solid tumors.

proposals.title: A Flux-Based Atlas of Metabolic Reprogramming by MCJ Silencing Across Human Immune Effector Lineages
proposals.motivation: Metabolic dysfunction is a critical barrier to effective anti-tumor immunity, limiting the persistence and function of engineered and endogenous immune cells. While MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein) is a known negative regulator of mitochondrial respiration whose silencing enhances T cell function, its pan-immune metabolic impact remains uncharacterized. A systematic, comparative analysis of metabolic flux following MCJ modulation across key immune lineages is needed to identify shared and cell-specific therapeutic nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: Silencing MCJ reprograms core metabolic pathways—including oxidative phosphorylation, glycolysis, and fatty acid oxidation—in a lineage-specific manner across human T cells, NK cells, and macrophages, and alleviating shared metabolic bottlenecks will synergistically enhance multi-arm immunotherapy.
proposals.method: We will employ stable isotope tracing (e.g., [U-13C]glucose, [U-13C]glutamine) coupled with mass spectrometry to quantify metabolic fluxes in primary human CAR-T cells, NK cells, monocyte-derived macrophages, and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) following MCJ knockdown via shRNA. Flux balance analysis (FBA) will be performed using the COBRA toolbox and context-specific genome-scale metabolic models (Recon3D) to compute intracellular reaction rates. Validation will use engineered mice with humanized immune systems and MCJ-deficient immune cells in syngeneic tumor models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, comparative map of metabolic rewiring by a single target across major immune effector lineages. It will identify universal metabolic limitations to anti-tumor immunity and lineage-specific dependencies, providing a rational blueprint for designing metabolic adjuvants and engineering strategies to improve CAR-T, NK cell, and macrophage-based therapies.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond single-cell-type metabolic profiling to a pan-immune flux atlas directly links a therapeutic target to a systems-level metabolic network, enabling the discovery of synergistic nodes for multi-cell immunotherapy, a strategy not yet explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Targeting MCJ to Reprogram Innate Immune Cell Metabolism for Enhanced Anti-Tumor Immunity
proposals.motivation: Immunotherapy efficacy is limited by the metabolic constraints of immune cells within the tumor microenvironment (TME). Pre-2026 work identified MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein) as a mitochondrial brake on T cell respiration; its silencing enhanced T cell function and tumor control. However, the role of MCJ in innate immune cells critical for anti-tumor immunity, such as Natural Killer (NK) cells and macrophages, remains unexplored. This gap is significant because these cells are pivotal for initiating and sustaining immune responses against solid tumors and hematologic cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic or pharmacologic silencing of MCJ in innate immune cells (NK cells and macrophages) will reprogram their mitochondrial metabolism, enhance effector functions, and overcome immunosuppressive barriers in the TME, leading to superior anti-tumor activity.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR-Cas9 to generate MCJ-deficient mice and use bone marrow chimeras to study cell-type-specific effects. Human immune cells will be isolated from healthy donors and cancer patients. MCJ will be silenced using siRNA or small-molecule inhibitors (developed pre-2026). Metabolic profiling (Seahorse, metabolomics) will assess oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis. Functional assays will measure NK cell cytotoxicity, macrophage polarization, and cytokine production. In vivo efficacy will be tested in syngeneic mouse tumor models (melanoma, lung cancer) and humanized mouse models engrafted with patient-derived tumors, integrating the lab's expertise in 'Engineering Mice to Study Human Immunity.'
proposals.contribution: This work will define MCJ as a novel pan-immunotherapy target beyond adaptive T cells, establishing its role in innate immune metabolism. It will provide pre-clinical proof-of-concept for MCJ inhibition as a strategy to enhance existing immunotherapies (e.g., checkpoint blockade) and treat cancers resistant to current T-cell-focused approaches. The findings will link mitochondrial regulation directly to innate anti-tumor effector functions.
proposals.novelty: While MCJ's role in T cells is known, its function in NK cells and macrophages is entirely unexplored. Extending this metabolic target to innate immunity represents a novel and synergistic approach to immunotherapy, moving beyond a single cell type to broadly remodel the immune landscape of the TME.

proposals.title: Targeting Mitochondrial Respiration via MCJ Overexpression to Restrain Pathogenic T Cells in Autoimmunity
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune diseases like multiple sclerosis and inflammatory bowel disease are driven by hyperactive, pathogenic T cells. Current immunosuppressive therapies are broad and carry significant side effects. Pre-2026 studies identify MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein/DnaJC15) as a natural brake on mitochondrial complex I and T cell activation, offering a novel metabolic checkpoint for selective immune modulation.
proposals.hypothesis: Overexpression of MCJ in antigen-specific T cells will limit their pathogenic potential by constraining mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, thereby ameliorating disease in preclinical models of autoimmunity without causing global immunosuppression.
proposals.method: We will engineer a conditional MCJ overexpression construct for adoptive transfer into the 'Engineering Mice to Study Human Immunity' platform. Using mouse models of experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE) and T cell-driven colitis, we will transfer MCJ-overexpressing versus control CD4+ T cells (e.g., Th1/Th17). Disease progression will be monitored clinically and histologically. We will employ Seahorse analysis and metabolomics (as in FABP5/ether lipid study) to quantify metabolic reprogramming, and use single-cell RNA-sequencing (integrated analysis akin to pulmonary carcinoma paper) to define resultant transcriptional states in pathogenic T cell subsets.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish MCJ as a viable therapeutic target for metabolic immune intervention, providing proof-of-concept that enhancing intrinsic metabolic brakes can treat autoimmunity. It will yield a detailed mechanistic map linking MCJ-driven mitochondrial restraint to T cell effector function and pathogenicity.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic reprogramming of T cells is known, leveraging the endogenous inhibitor MCJ via overexpression to specifically dampen pathogenic subsets is a novel and untested strategy, distinct from general nutrient deprivation or pharmacological complex I inhibition.

proposals.title: Compensatory EGFR Ligand Networks in Skin Fibrosis: Defining the Pathogenic Axis Beyond EREG
proposals.motivation: Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) signaling is a key driver of tissue fibrosis, with Epiregulin (EREG) implicated as a primary pathogenic ligand. However, clinical neutralization of single ligands often fails due to pathway redundancy, a major hurdle in fibrotic disease therapy. This proposal addresses the critical gap in understanding the compensatory ligand network that sustains pathogenic EGFR signaling in the skin, directly relevant to scleroderma and keloid scarring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neutralization of EREG in dermal fibrosis will trigger the compensatory upregulation of specific alternative EGFR ligands (e.g., Amphiregulin, HB-EGF, TGF-α), which will sustain fibroblast activation and collagen production.
proposals.method: We will employ a humanized mouse model of bleomycin-induced skin fibrosis, engineered to study human immune and stromal cell interactions. Mice will be treated with an anti-EREG neutralizing antibody. Fibrotic lesions will be analyzed by high-parameter spectral flow cytometry (Cytek Aurora) and single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) to map cellular sources and expression dynamics of all seven EGFR ligands. Bulk RNA-seq from human scleroderma patient biopsies (pre-2026 cohorts) will be integrated to validate ligand expression patterns using DESeq2 and weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA).
proposals.contribution: This work will define the hierarchy and redundancy within the EGFR ligand family in fibrotic skin disease, identifying the key compensatory ligands that sustain signaling. It will provide a mechanistic rationale for combination or sequential targeting strategies to durably inhibit pro-fibrotic EGFR signaling, moving beyond single-ligand approaches.
proposals.novelty: While EREG's role is established, the systematic in vivo identification of its compensatory network in a human-relevant fibrotic microenvironment is novel. Integrating a humanized mouse model with deep molecular profiling of patient tissues bridges the gap between mechanistic discovery and human disease pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Defining the Cellular Source of Epiregulin in Skin Fibrosis: A Test of DC3 Exclusivity
proposals.motivation: Epiregulin (EREG), an EGFR ligand, is a key pathogenic driver in fibrotic skin diseases like systemic sclerosis. While a subset of dendritic cells (DC3s) has been implicated as a source, the potential contribution of other resident or infiltrating cell types remains unexplored. Determining the complete cellular repertoire of pathogenic EREG production is critical for developing precise, cell-targeted anti-fibrotic therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DC3s are the sole, non-redundant cellular source of pathogenic EREG in fibrotic skin, and that other candidate cell types, including fibroblasts, macrophages, and T cells, do not produce EREG at levels sufficient to drive disease.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-modal approach using pre-2026 technologies. First, we will generate a novel EREG-reporter/fate-mapping mouse (Ereg-CreERT2;Rosa26-tdTomato) to lineage-trace EREG-expressing cells during bleomycin-induced skin fibrosis. Second, we will perform high-parameter single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq, 10x Genomics) and CITE-seq on fibrotic skin, analyzing data with Seurat and SCENIC to identify all EREG+ clusters and their regulatory states. Third, we will validate findings using spatial transcriptomics (Visium) and in situ hybridization, and test necessity using cell-type-specific EREG conditional knockout mice (e.g., using LysM-Cre, Col1a2-Cre).
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the cellular landscape of EREG production in fibrosis, establishing whether DC3s are a unique therapeutic target. It will provide a high-resolution molecular profile of EREG+ cells and their niche, advancing our understanding of cytokine source specificity in tissue remodeling.
proposals.novelty: While DC3s are known to produce EREG, systematically testing the exclusivity of this source across all major skin cell types in fibrosis is novel. Combining fate-mapping, single-cell multi-omics, and spatial analysis to address this question represents a significant technical and conceptual advance over prior correlative studies.

proposals.title: Tenascin-C Drives Fibrogenesis in Systemic Sclerosis via TLR4-Independent Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Systemic sclerosis (SSc) is a devastating fibrotic disease with no cure. The matricellular protein Tenascin-C (TNC) is highly upregulated in SSc skin and promotes inflammation via TLR4, but its direct role in fibrosis remains unclear. Determining if TNC drives fibrosis independently of its inflammatory TLR4 axis is critical for developing targeted anti-fibrotic therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TNC promotes fibroblast activation and collagen deposition in scleroderma through mechanisms that are independent of its canonical TLR4 signaling pathway.
proposals.method: We will utilize primary human dermal fibroblasts from SSc patients and healthy controls, alongside TNC-knockout and TLR4-knockout mice in the bleomycin-induced skin fibrosis model. We will employ CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing in human fibroblasts to create TNC, TLR4, and double-knockout lines. Fibrogenic responses (α-SMA, COL1A1 expression, collagen gel contraction) will be assessed upon TNC stimulation. In vivo, disease progression will be compared in wild-type, Tnc-/-, Tlr4-/-, and double-knockout mice using histology, hydroxyproline content, and single-cell RNA sequencing of skin to map fibroblast activation states.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively dissect the TLR4-dependent and -independent roles of TNC in fibrosis, identifying novel therapeutic targets downstream of TNC. It will provide a high-resolution map of fibroblast heterogeneity in SSc driven by specific matrix signals, advancing our mechanistic understanding of the disease.
proposals.novelty: While TNC-TLR4 in inflammation is known, its TLR4-independent pro-fibrotic actions are unexplored. This proposal uniquely leverages Flavell's expertise in humanized immunology and genetic mouse models to deconvolute these matrix-driven pathways in a clinically relevant context.

proposals.title: Serum and Skin Tenascin-C as a Predictive Biomarker for Disease Activity and Therapeutic Response to Anti-EREG Therapy in Inflammatory Skin Disease
proposals.motivation: Epiregulin (EREG) is a key epithelial-derived cytokine implicated in inflammatory skin diseases like atopic dermatitis and psoriasis, driving immune cell activation and tissue remodeling. Anti-EREG therapies are emerging, but robust, non-invasive biomarkers to stratify patients and monitor treatment response are lacking. Tenascin-C (TNC), a matricellular protein induced by inflammation and tissue injury, is a potential downstream effector of EREG signaling, making it a compelling candidate biomarker.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TNC expression in skin and serum levels will correlate with disease severity and will dynamically decrease in response to anti-EREG therapy, serving as a pharmacodynamic and predictive biomarker.
proposals.method: We will utilize a pre-clinical mouse model of atopic dermatitis (e.g., MC903-induced) and humanized immune system mice engineered to study human skin inflammation. Mice will be treated with a neutralizing anti-EREG monoclonal antibody. Disease activity will be scored clinically and histologically. TNC levels will be quantified longitudinally in serum by ELISA and in skin lesions by immunohistochemistry and RNAscope for spatial gene expression analysis. Correlation and regression analyses will link TNC metrics to disease scores and treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish TNC as a novel, mechanistically grounded biomarker for EREG-driven pathology, providing a tool for patient selection and early efficacy readouts in clinical trials. It will also advance our understanding of the EREG-TNC axis in immune-epithelial crosstalk during skin inflammation.
proposals.novelty: While TNC is known in inflammation, its specific link to EREG biology in skin disease and its validation as a therapy-responsive biomarker is unexplored. The integrated use of engineered mouse models and human tissue correlates bridges translational gaps in biomarker discovery.

proposals.title: Clonally Expanded T Cells in Sclerotic Skin Diseases: A High-Resolution TCR Sequencing Study to Identify Antigen-Driven Immune Responses
proposals.motivation: Sclerotic skin diseases like systemic sclerosis and morphea are characterized by fibrosis and inflammation, but the specific T-cell antigens driving the pathogenic immune response remain largely unknown. This gap hinders the development of targeted immunotherapies. The success of T-cell receptor (TCR) sequencing in identifying tumor-reactive clones in oncology provides a powerful, untapped methodology to apply to these autoimmune fibrotic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lesional skin from patients with sclerotic diseases harbors oligoclonal, antigen-driven expansions of T cells, and that the TCR repertoires of these expanded clones will be shared across patients, pointing to common autoantigens.
proposals.method: We will perform high-throughput TCRβ sequencing (Adaptive Biotechnologies ImmunoSEQ platform) on sorted CD4+ and CD8+ T cells from paired lesional skin, unaffected skin, and peripheral blood of patients with systemic sclerosis and morphea, alongside healthy controls. Clonal expansion will be quantified using the Shannon entropy index and clonality metrics. Public TCR sequences (shared across individuals) will be identified using the GLIPH2 algorithm, and their predicted antigen specificity will be inferred by cross-referencing with public databases like VDJdb and McPAS-TCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the landscape of antigen-driven T-cell responses in sclerotic skin, potentially identifying shared pathogenic TCRs and their inferred antigenic targets. It will establish a foundational immune repertoire map for these diseases, directly informing the development of antigen-specific diagnostics and therapeutic strategies, such as TCR-based biomarkers or engineered T-cell therapies.
proposals.novelty: While TCR sequencing is established in oncology, its systematic application to dissect antigen specificity in fibrotic autoimmune skin diseases is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive cytokine profiling to directly probe the clonal architecture and potential antigenic drivers of sclerosis.

proposals.title: Modeling Immune-Fibroblast Crosstalk in Systemic Sclerosis Using a Human Skin-on-a-Chip Platform
proposals.motivation: Systemic sclerosis (SSc) is a devastating fibrotic autoimmune disease with no cure, characterized by dysfunctional immune cell activation and pathological fibroblast differentiation leading to skin and organ fibrosis. Current models, primarily murine or 2D cell culture, fail to recapitulate the dynamic, multicellular human tissue microenvironment and fluid flow conditions critical for disease progression. There is an urgent need for human-relevant, tractable models to dissect disease mechanisms and identify therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microfluidic organ-chip model incorporating primary human dermal fibroblasts, keratinocytes, and autologous immune cells will recapitulate key features of SSc skin fibrosis, and that specific immune-derived signals (e.g., from T cells or macrophages) are necessary and sufficient to drive pathological fibroblast activation and extracellular matrix deposition in this system.
proposals.method: We will engineer a multi-channel microfluidic skin-on-a-chip device featuring a porous membrane separating a vascular channel (seeded with human dermal microvascular endothelial cells and peripheral blood mononuclear cells from SSc patients or healthy donors) from a dermal compartment (seeded with primary human dermal fibroblasts and overlayed with keratinocytes). Using time-lapse imaging, bulk RNA-seq, and multiplex cytokine profiling, we will quantify fibroblast activation (α-SMA, COL1A1), immune cell infiltration, and matrix stiffness in response to patient-derived immune cells or defined cytokine cocktails (e.g., TGF-β, IL-4, IL-13). Genetic perturbation (e.g., CRISPRi knockdown of PRMTs in fibroblasts or immune cells) will be used to validate mechanistic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, physiologically relevant human model of SSc skin fibrosis that integrates key immune and stromal components. It will directly identify the critical immune-derived soluble and cellular factors that initiate and sustain fibroblast activation. The platform will serve as a high-content, patient-specific tool for preclinical testing of anti-fibrotic and immunomodulatory drugs.
proposals.novelty: While organ-chips exist for skin, none have been specifically engineered to model the complex immune-stromal interactions of autoimmune fibrosis using patient-derived cells. This approach moves beyond static co-cultures by incorporating physiological flow and mechanical cues, and leverages Dr. Flavell's expertise in human immunology and genetic engineering ('Engineering Mice to Study Human Immunity') in a reductionist human system.

proposals.title: Dual Inhibition of EREG and TLR4 Signaling as a Novel Therapeutic Strategy for Skin Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Fibrotic skin diseases, such as systemic sclerosis, are characterized by excessive extracellular matrix deposition and have limited treatment options. Pre-2026 research implicates the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) ligand epiregulin (EREG) in fibroblast activation and fibrosis, while its downstream signaling can converge on Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)-mediated pro-fibrotic and inflammatory responses. Simultaneously targeting these two interconnected pathways may offer superior efficacy by blocking both a key initiator and a critical amplifier of fibrotic signaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combined pharmacological blockade of EREG and TLR4 will synergistically inhibit fibroblast activation and collagen production, leading to a significantly enhanced anti-fibrotic effect compared to single-agent therapy in models of skin fibrosis.
proposals.method: We will utilize a bleomycin-induced skin fibrosis model in humanized mice engineered to study human immune responses. Mice will be treated with an anti-EREG monoclonal antibody, a small-molecule TLR4 inhibitor (TAK-242), or the combination. Fibrosis will be assessed histologically (Masson's trichrome) and by quantifying hydroxyproline content. Single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics platform) of skin lesions will be performed to define the cellular and molecular impact of dual inhibition on fibroblast subsets, immune cell infiltration, and fibrotic gene expression networks (e.g., COL1A1, ACTA2).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the therapeutic potential and mechanistic basis for a novel dual-target approach in fibrosis. It will delineate the specific immune and stromal cell populations modulated by EREG/TLR4 inhibition and provide a pre-clinical rationale for a new combination therapy for fibrotic skin diseases.
proposals.novelty: While EREG or TLR4 have been studied individually in inflammation, their specific cooperative role in skin fibrosis is unexplored. The proposal is novel in testing their combined inhibition as a synergistic strategy, leveraging Flavell's expertise in humanized mouse models and immunology to dissect the integrated immune-stromal axis in fibrosis.

proposals.title: An Integrated Regulatory Code for cGAS Activation: Defining the Combinatorial Rules of DNA Sequence, Epigenetic Modification, and Cellular Compartmentalization
proposals.motivation: The cGAS-STING pathway is a critical cytosolic DNA sensor for antiviral defense and is implicated in autoinflammatory diseases and cancer. However, a fundamental gap exists in understanding how cGAS distinguishes immunogenic 'non-self' or damaged DNA from tolerated 'self' DNA, as sequence and location alone are insufficient predictors. Defining the molecular features that constitute this discriminatory code is essential for understanding immune homeostasis and developing targeted therapies for cGAS-related pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cGAS activation is governed by an integrated regulatory code where specific DNA structural motifs and epigenetic modifications combinatorially determine binding affinity and enzymatic activity, and that this intrinsic code is critically modulated by compartment-specific regulatory proteins and enzymes.
proposals.method: We will employ a reductionist biochemical screen using purified human cGAS and a library of synthetic DNA oligonucleotides containing defined sequence motifs (Z-DNA, cruciforms, AT-rich) and epigenetic marks (5mC, 5hmC, 8-oxoG) to quantify binding and cGAMP production. We will then validate and contextualize these findings in cellular systems, using engineered mouse and human cell lines (including primary keratinocytes and immune cells) to test how compartment-specific factors (e.g., TREX1 nuclease, PQBP1, Beclin-1) alter the immunogenicity of DNA bearing these features. Spatial proteomics and CRISPR-based perturbation of candidate regulators will link specific compartments to code interpretation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first predictive framework for cGAS immunogenicity based on combinatorial DNA features, moving beyond a binary 'cytosolic DNA' model. It will directly link specific epigenetic states, such as those found in cancer or senescent cells, to innate immune activation potential. The findings will provide a mechanistic basis for cGAS-dependent autoimmunity and suggest new strategies for modulating the pathway in disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined cGAS activation by DNA length or location in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates the influence of specific DNA chemistry and structure with the physiological context of cellular compartmentalization, testing their non-additive, combinatorial effects on immune signaling.

proposals.title: Tonic cGAS-STING signaling enforces peripheral T cell tolerance through cell-intrinsic and -extrinsic mechanisms
proposals.motivation: The cGAS-STING pathway is a canonical sensor of cytosolic DNA, driving potent inflammatory and anti-tumor responses. However, its role in immune homeostasis remains poorly defined. Pre-2026 work suggests low-level, tonic STING signaling may exist, but its immunological function, particularly in maintaining T cell tolerance to prevent autoimmunity, is unknown and represents a critical gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tonic, low-level cGAS activity, distinct from its inflammatory activation, provides a critical homeostatic signal required for the establishment and maintenance of peripheral T cell tolerance.
proposals.method: We will generate inducible, cell-type-specific cGAS knockout mice (using CreERT2 systems) in T cells (Cd4-Cre) and antigen-presenting cells (Cd11c-Cre). Adult mice will be treated with tamoxifen to ablate cGAS, avoiding developmental compensation. We will employ high-dimensional flow cytometry (30+ parameters) and single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics) to profile T cell subsets, activation states, and transcriptional programs. Functional tolerance will be assessed using in vivo T cell priming assays with self-antigen mimics and ex vivo suppression assays for Treg function.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel homeostatic function for the cGAS pathway in immune regulation, moving beyond its inflammatory role. It will establish whether tonic cGAS signaling is a non-redundant checkpoint against autoimmunity and identify the key cellular compartments (T cell-intrinsic vs. APC-extrinsic) involved. This could reveal new therapeutic targets for autoimmune diseases and inform the use of STING agonists in cancer.
proposals.novelty: This proposal challenges the prevailing view of cGAS solely as an inflammatory trigger by testing its essential role in tolerance, a concept supported by scant pre-2026 evidence. The use of adult-inducible knockouts to isolate tonic signaling from developmental effects is a novel and critical technical approach.

proposals.title: Tonic cGAS-STING signaling calibrates immune tolerance: Defining a dose-dependent rheostat in skin-resident immune cells
proposals.motivation: The cGAS-STING pathway is a critical sensor of cytosolic DNA, and its hyperactivation is strongly implicated in autoimmune and inflammatory diseases like lupus and Aicardi-Goutières syndrome. However, pre-2026 research has largely treated cGAS as a binary alarm, focusing on its pathological overactivation. This overlooks the possibility that, like many immune receptors, it may have essential physiological functions at baseline. Understanding this could reveal new therapeutic strategies that modulate, rather than completely inhibit, the pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-level, tonic cGAS signaling in skin-resident immune cells (e.g., Langerhans cells, dermal dendritic cells) provides a homeostatic signal that calibrates their activation threshold, promoting a tolerogenic state and preventing aberrant inflammation.
proposals.method: We will engineer a novel mouse model with a Cre-inducible, titratable expression system for a catalytically active cGAS mutant, allowing precise control of cGAS activity in specific cell lineages (e.g., using Langerin-Cre). Using this model, we will establish dose-response curves by exposing primary cells and skin explants to varying levels of cytosolic DNA or cGAS activity. We will employ single-cell RNA-sequencing and ATAC-seq to map the transcriptional and epigenetic landscapes distinguishing tonic from burst signaling. Functional validation will involve assessing antigen presentation capacity, T cell priming, and susceptibility to inflammatory skin challenges (e.g., imiquimod-induced dermatitis) across signaling gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular signature and functional consequences of physiological cGAS signaling, moving beyond the binary 'on/off' paradigm. It will establish a conceptual framework of the cGAS rheostat, identifying potential therapeutic windows for modulating the pathway in autoimmune and inflammatory skin diseases without causing immunosuppression.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel by directly applying the excitotoxicity analogy from neuroscience to immune sensing, testing the non-obvious idea that a known driver of autoimmunity has a necessary homeostatic function. It innovates methodologically by moving from global knockouts to precise, titratable gain-of-function models to dissect dose-dependent effects.

proposals.title: Targeted Nanoparticle Delivery of MCJ siRNA to Modulate Mitochondrial Metabolism and cGAS-STING Signaling for the Treatment of Autoimmune Dermatitis
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune and inflammatory skin diseases like atopic dermatitis involve dysregulated immune responses where metabolic dysfunction in immune cells contributes to pathology. Pre-2026 work has established that mitochondrial respiration influences immune cell fate and that the cGAS-STING pathway, while critical for antiviral defense, can drive detrimental inflammation when chronically activated by self-DNA. Current systemic immunosuppressants lack cellular specificity and carry significant side effects, highlighting the need for targeted, transient immunomodulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transient, targeted inhibition of the mitochondrial protein MCJ in skin-infiltrating immune cells will enhance oxidative phosphorylation, thereby modulating the cGAS-STING pathway to suppress pro-inflammatory signaling and promote a regulatory, tolerance-promoting state in models of autoimmune dermatitis.
proposals.method: We will engineer biodegradable poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles conjugated with a CD11c-targeting ligand for delivery to dendritic cells. These nanoparticles will encapsulate siRNA against MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J-protein). Using established mouse models of atopic dermatitis (e.g., MC903-induced) and autoimmune-prone backgrounds, we will assess nanoparticle biodistribution, MCJ knockdown efficiency, and changes in mitochondrial respiration via Seahorse analysis. Downstream effects on cGAS-STING activity (phospho-TBK1/IRF3, IFN-β), cytokine profiles, T cell polarization, and disease pathology will be quantified. Human immune cell assays will validate metabolic and signaling effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between targeted mitochondrial metabolic reprogramming and the functional output of the cGAS-STING pathway in autoimmune pathology. It will provide proof-of-concept for a novel, non-genetic nanotherapeutic strategy to restore immune tolerance by metabolically 're-educating' autoreactive cells, moving beyond broad immunosuppression.
proposals.novelty: While metabolic manipulation of immunity is known, the use of transient, cell-targeted nanoparticle delivery to specifically modulate mitochondrial respiration for the purpose of fine-tuning cGAS-STING signaling toward tolerance is a novel and untested therapeutic paradigm. This approach merges precise immunometabolic targeting with controlled nucleic acid delivery.

proposals.title: CRISPR Activation Screening in cGAS-STING-Null Macrophages Identifies and Validates Novel Cytosolic DNA Sensors Driving Antiviral Immunity
proposals.motivation: The cGAS-STING pathway is the canonical cytosolic DNA sensor, but pre-2026 evidence suggests residual immune responses to DNA in its absence, hinting at alternative sensors. Defining these pathways is critical for understanding antiviral and antitumor immunity, especially in contexts where cGAS-STING is suppressed by pathogens or in specific tissues. This project directly addresses this gap to provide a more complete picture of innate immune sensing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, genetically encoded proteins beyond cGAS can directly bind cytosolic DNA and initiate innate immune signaling, and that these can be identified through a gain-of-function genetic screen in a cGAS-STING-null background.
proposals.method: We will engineer cGAS/STING double-knockout immortalized bone marrow-derived macrophages (iBMDMs) from mice. Using a genome-wide CRISPR activation (CRISPRa) library (e.g., SAM), we will screen for genes whose overexpression restores IFN-β production upon transfection with immunostimulatory DNA (ISD). Hit genes will be validated by CRISPR knockout to assess necessity, followed by recombinant protein expression for in vitro DNA-binding assays (EMSA). Finally, we will test the in vivo relevance of top candidates using AAV-mediated gene delivery in cGas/Sting double-KO mice challenged with DNA viruses (e.g., HSV-1).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, functionally validated list of non-canonical cytosolic DNA sensors. It will establish new nodes in the innate immune signaling network and reveal potential therapeutic targets for modulating immunity in diseases like cancer or viral infection where cGAS-STING activity is compromised.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies noted cGAS-independent responses, this is a systematic, hypothesis-driven, gain-of-function genetic screen designed explicitly to discover the effectors of these pathways, moving beyond correlative observations to mechanistic definition.

proposals.title: A Tissue-Resolved Single-Cell Atlas of Redundant Cytosolic DNA Sensor Networks in Homeostasis and Inflammation
proposals.motivation: The cGAS-STING pathway is a major cytosolic DNA sensor, but other sensors like AIM2, IFI16, and DDX41 exist and are implicated in autoimmunity, cancer, and skin diseases like psoriasis. Pre-2026 work has largely studied these pathways in isolation, leaving a critical gap in understanding their redundant, cell-type-specific, and tissue-specific orchestration in vivo. A systems-level map is needed to explain how collective DNA sensor activity dictates inflammatory outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, cGAS-independent transcriptional modules exist across tissues and cell types, revealing the functional activation of alternative DNA sensors, and that these modules define unique cellular states that drive tissue-specific inflammatory pathology.
proposals.method: We will generate a single-cell RNA-seq atlas from immune and non-immune cells (epithelial, stromal) across skin, lung, and spleen in wild-type and cGAS-deficient mice, with and without cytosolic DNA challenge (e.g., transfected ISD, HSV-1 infection). Using Seurat for integration and clustering, we will computationally deconvolve cGAS-dependent and -independent response modules. SCENIC will infer regulon activity to identify master regulators of alternative sensor pathways. Genetic perturbations (CRISPR/Cas9 in primary cells or conditional knockouts) of candidate sensors (AIM2, IFI16) in atlas-identified key cell types (e.g., keratinocytes, macrophages) will validate functional necessity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, tissue-resolved map of redundant cytosolic DNA sensing networks, linking specific sensor modules to distinct cellular states. It will establish a foundational resource for understanding how DNA-driven inflammation is fine-tuned across tissues, directly informing mechanisms in diseases like cutaneous lupus, pulmonary fibrosis, and cancer immunotherapy responses.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the dominant cGAS-STING paradigm, we systematically map the *in vivo* activity of the entire DNA sensor network at single-cell resolution across tissues, integrating computational deconvolution with genetic validation in specific cellular contexts identified by the atlas.

ideas.title: Defining the dose-dependent role of tonic cGAS signaling in enforcing peripheral T cell tolerance
ideas.description: This study investigates the hypothesis that low-level, tonic cGAS activity, distinct from inflammatory activation, is a critical homeostatic signal required for the establishment and maintenance of peripheral T cell tolerance. Using an inducible, cell-type-specific knockout system, it will dissect the consequences of ablating cGAS signaling specifically in T cells or antigen-presenting cells in adult mice, avoiding developmental compensation. The research will integrate high-dimensional immunophenotyping, transcriptional profiling, and functional assays to determine if the absence of this baseline signal leads to a breakdown in tolerance mechanisms, such as increased autoreactive T cell priming or impaired regulatory T cell function. This approach directly tests a potential beneficial role for cGAS while accounting for the context and intensity of its activation.

This approach directly tests if MCJ is a critical metabolic determinant that differentially guides the fate and function of distinct T cell lineages, moving beyond observational correlation to establish mechanism.

ideas.title: Preclinical mechanistic evaluation of MCJ-silenced CAR T-cell metabolism and function in humanized models prior to clinical trial design
ideas.description: This redesigned study first engineers and validates CAR T-cells with and without MCJ silencing using identical vector architectures. These cells are then comprehensively evaluated in vitro for metabolic parameters (mitochondrial function, glycolysis) and in vivo using humanized mouse models with relevant human tumors. The study employs single-cell RNA sequencing, ATAC-seq, and metabolomic profiling to map the precise molecular and functional consequences of MCJ silencing on CAR T-cell persistence, exhaustion, and anti-tumor efficacy. This data-driven, mechanistic foundation is used to define safety biomarkers and optimize the final CAR construct design before proceeding to a human clinical trial.

ideas.title: Transient MCJ inhibition via nanoparticles to modulate cGAS activity for immune tolerance restoration
ideas.description: This research direction proposes developing biodegradable nanoparticles for the transient, tissue-targeted delivery of MCJ-silencing agents to immune cells. The goal is to temporarily enhance mitochondrial respiration, which may modulate the activity and signaling output of the cGAS-STING pathway. By doing so, we aim to shift low-level cGAS activity from a detrimental, pro-inflammatory state (as seen in autoimmunity) toward a beneficial, regulatory function that promotes immune tolerance. This approach allows for the metabolic 're-education' of autoreactive cells to restore homeostasis, offering a controlled, non-genetic intervention for autoimmune diseases.

ideas.title: Single-cell atlas of redundant cytosolic DNA sensor activation states across tissues
ideas.description: This project will use high-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing to profile immune and non-immune cells across multiple tissues, capturing their complete transcriptional response to cytosolic DNA. By analyzing cells with known cGAS-STING activation, we will computationally identify distinct, cGAS-independent gene expression modules that signify the activity of alternative DNA sensors like AIM2, IFI16, or DDX41. This atlas will map where and in which cell types these redundant pathways are operational, functionally testing their necessity through genetic perturbations in specific cellular contexts identified by the atlas. The result is a systems-level understanding of how the network of DNA sensors collectively shapes tissue-specific immune responses and inflammatory disease states.

ideas.title: Subset-specific nanoparticle delivery for transient MCJ inhibition to differentially modulate human T cell immunity
ideas.description: This research direction combines the need to understand MCJ's subset-specific roles with a targeted, transient delivery technology. It involves engineering biodegradable nanoparticles functionalized with ligands (e.g., antibodies, aptamers) to selectively deliver MCJ-silencing RNA (siRNA or ASO) to distinct human T cell populations (CD4+, Treg, memory) in vitro and in vivo. This allows for the direct comparison of how temporary MCJ inhibition differentially affects metabolism, function, and persistence of each subset, revealing unique therapeutic windows. Ultimately, this platform can be used to test whether transient metabolic reprogramming of specific subsets can enhance anti-tumor immunity, modulate autoimmunity, or improve vaccine responses in a controlled and reversible manner.

ideas.title: Determine if patient-specific MCJ expression dictates CAR-T metabolic fitness and durability
ideas.description: This study investigates the hypothesis that baseline MCJ expression in a patient's T cells determines their susceptibility to metabolic stress upon CAR engineering, thereby predicting clinical outcomes. It will functionally test if T cells from patients with low vs. high MCJ exhibit differential metabolic imbalance and ROS production following chronic in vitro CAR stimulation, mimicking therapeutic pressure. This directly links the correlative observation (Idea B) to the mechanistic cause (Idea A). The synergy is a precision medicine strategy: identifying patients whose CAR-T products are metabolically compromised by low MCJ, who may benefit from adjunct antioxidant or metabolic support therapies to improve durability and prevent relapse.

ideas.title: Assess long-term persistence of MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells using ecological population monitoring techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology and wildlife population biology to the problem of monitoring the long-term persistence, exhaustion, and toxicity of MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells. In ecology, the sustainability and impact of reintroduced or engineered species are assessed through integrated methods: direct individual tagging for lineage and movement, non-invasive sampling for population health and genetics, and broad environmental monitoring for unintended effects. Adapted to this research context, the solution is to implement a multi-modal, long-term in vivo tracking system. This combines stable genetic barcoding of the CAR-T cell product for precise clonal durability tracking, serial and minimally invasive sampling of peripheral blood and bone marrow (using high-throughput sequencing and metabolic profiling) to dynamically assess exhaustion states and functional adaptation, and comprehensive longitudinal monitoring of the host immune landscape and end-organ health to evaluate potential chronic toxicity or immunopathology, providing a holistic assessment of the engineered cell population's long-term fate and safety.

ideas.title: Pan-Immune Metabolic Reprogramming: A Flux-Based Atlas of MCJ Silencing Across Immune Cell Lineages
ideas.description: This research direction integrates detailed metabolic flux analysis (MFA) with the pan-immunotherapy targeting of MCJ. It applies advanced MFA techniques—such as isotope tracing and computational modeling—not just to CAR-T cells, but systematically to NK cells, macrophages, and TILs following MCJ silencing. This generates a high-resolution, comparative map of metabolic rewiring across key immune effector lineages. The synergy lies in using this cross-cell atlas to identify universal metabolic bottlenecks whose alleviation could enhance multiple immune cell types simultaneously, and to pinpoint cell-specific metabolic dependencies that inform tailored engineering or adjuvant strategies for synergistic, multi-arm immunotherapies.

ideas.title: Patient-Specific Metabolic Profiling to Guide Synergistic MCJ Silencing Combinations in the Tumor Microenvironment
ideas.description: This redesigned study moves beyond simple combinatorial screening by integrating patient-specific tumor models. It utilizes a library of patient-derived organoids (PDOs) co-cultured with autologous immune cells (CAR-Ts and TILs) to create a more physiologically relevant testing platform. The system first profiles the baseline immunometabolic state of each PDO-immune cell pair, then tests the efficacy of MCJ knockdown combined with various metabolic modulators. This approach aims to identify not just generic synergies, but predictive biomarkers and metabolic subtypes of tumors that are uniquely susceptible to specific combination therapies, thereby enabling a precision medicine strategy for immunometabolic intervention.

ideas.title: The cGAS Rheostat: Investigating a Dose-Dependent Role in Immune Homeostasis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cGAS activation is always detrimental in autoimmunity by drawing an analogy from neuroscience's excitotoxicity paradigm. In neuroscience, it was discovered that while excessive NMDA receptor glutamate signaling is pathological, a baseline level is crucial for healthy neuronal function. Adapting this solution, we propose investigating cGAS activity not as a binary switch but as a rheostat. The research would seek to determine if low-level, tonic cGAS signaling—below the threshold for robust type I interferon production—has beneficial homeostatic or regulatory functions, such as calibrating immune cell responsiveness, promoting tolerogenic states, or maintaining cellular fitness. This involves mapping the dose-response relationship of cytosolic DNA to identify the molecular and functional outputs that distinguish physiological 'tonic' signaling from pathological 'burst' signaling in the context of immune tolerance.

ideas.title: Systematically evaluate the impact of solid tumor microenvironments on the long-term persistence and functional durability of MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells
ideas.description: This study integrates long-term in vivo persistence tracking from Idea A with the expansion to multiple solid tumor types from Idea B. It will longitudinally monitor MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells across diverse solid tumor models (e.g., pancreatic, lung, glioblastoma) to compare their durability, exhaustion trajectories, and toxicity profiles in each unique microenvironment. The synergy lies in correlating specific tumor-intrinsic factors (e.g., metabolic landscape, immunosuppressive signals) with the rate of CAR-T functional decline, generating a map of which cancers benefit most from MCJ targeting. This will guide rational combination therapies tailored to overcome the dominant exhaustion pathways in each tumor type.

Top hits will be rigorously validated by assessing their necessity (via knockout), direct DNA-binding, and ability to reconstitute signaling in non-immune cells, ultimately testing their role in anti-viral or anti-tumor responses in vivo.

The analogous solution involves moving beyond a single biomarker (MCJ expression) to create a multivariate 'T-cell Fitness Index' that captures the functional heterogeneity of a patient's pre-infusion T-cell product. By integrating MCJ levels with other metrics of mitochondrial health and cellular fitness, this adapted approach aims to provide a more robust and predictive model of patient-specific therapy response and relapse risk than any single parameter could achieve.

ideas.title: Decoding the cGAS Regulatory Code: How Sequence and Epigenetic Context Integrate with Cellular Compartmentalization to Govern Immune Activation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the hypothesis that the immunogenicity of cytosolic DNA is not determined solely by its location or its sequence/modifications in isolation, but by an integrated 'regulatory code' where specific DNA structural and chemical features are interpreted by the cellular cGAS regulatory machinery. We will systematically test how known sequence motifs (e.g., Z-DNA, cruciforms) and epigenetic modifications (e.g., cytosine methylation, oxidized bases) influence cGAS binding and activation potency, and crucially, how this influence is modulated by compartment-specific factors like nucleases, helicases, and inhibitory proteins (e.g., PQBP1, Beclin-1). The goal is to establish a predictive framework that defines the combinatorial rules which allow the cell to distinguish 'self' from 'non-self' or 'damaged self' DNA based on its molecular signature and context, thereby preventing autoimmunity while maintaining robust pathogen defense.

ideas.title: Apply cryo-EM to visualize CAR-T cell cGAS activation and design MCJ-silencing constructs
ideas.description: This research direction uses high-resolution cryo-electron microscopy to capture the structural dynamics of the cGAS-DNA complex specifically within human CAR-T cells, identifying how tonic signaling or endogenous DNA sensing leads to dysfunction. These structural insights are then directly applied to inform the design of novel CAR constructs that incorporate elements for targeted silencing of MCJ (Methylation-Controlled J protein), a mitochondrial protein linked to metabolic fitness and exhaustion. The goal is to create a unified platform where structural biology guides the engineering of armored CAR-T cells that are resistant to cGAS-mediated exhaustion pathways, thereby improving efficacy and safety in clinical applications.

ideas.title: Adapting RNAi Delivery Strategies from Plant Virology for MCJ-Silencing CAR T-Cell Engineering
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from plant virology, where attenuated viruses are used to spread RNAi triggers systemically to confer antiviral resistance, this idea adapts that delivery solution for human CAR T-cell engineering. The analogous problem of achieving efficient, widespread, yet transient gene silencing without genomic integration is addressed by using a non-integrating, replicating RNA viral vector to deliver MCJ-silencing machinery (shRNA or CRISPRi) to T-cells ex vivo. This adapted strategy for clinical trial evaluation aims to transiently silence MCJ to boost mitochondrial function in CAR T-cells, potentially improving their efficacy and persistence in cancer patients, while mitigating long-term safety concerns associated with permanent genetic modification.

ideas.title: Adapting Spatial Metabolomics Workflows to Map cGAS Activation States in Tissue Context
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from spatial metabolomics in plant biology, where laser-capture microdissection (LCM) is used to isolate cells from specific tissue regions of interest prior to deep molecular profiling. The analogous problem is linking a localized stimulus to heterogeneous cellular responses. Here, we adapt this by applying LCM to capture cells from defined histological niches relevant to innate immunity (e.g., sites of microbial invasion, regions of tumor necrosis, or areas of autoimmune lesion). These spatially annotated cell pools are then subjected to single-cell RNA sequencing to profile the transcriptional outputs of the cGAS-STING pathway. This combined spatial-transcriptional map will reveal how a cell's precise tissue microenvironment and proximity to a damage signal shapes its specific cGAS activation state, moving beyond a bulk-tissue or dissociated-cell understanding of this pathway.

ideas.title: Applying Allosteric Control Principles from Enzymology to Modulate cGAS Activity
ideas.description: This research idea applies the principle of allosteric regulation from enzymology to the problem of controlling cGAS activity. In enzymology, precise control is often achieved through regulatory molecules binding at sites distinct from the active site, inducing conformational changes that modulate function. We propose to adapt this solution by systematically searching for endogenous cellular factors (e.g., metabolites, proteins, or specific modifications) that act as allosteric regulators of cGAS. Discovering and characterizing these regulators and their binding pockets will reveal novel cellular mechanisms for the fine-tuned, reversible control of cGAS, preventing aberrant activation and autoimmunity, and could inform the design of new therapeutic modulators.

ideas.title: Functional Metabolic Flux Interrogation via Integrated CRISPRi and Real-time Biosensing in MCJ-deficient CAR-T Cells
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond descriptive mapping to establish causal links between metabolic flux and function. It employs a dual-component system: first, a CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) library targeting key metabolic nodes is introduced into MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells to create a panel of defined metabolic perturbations. Second, these engineered cells are subjected to continuous metabolic flux analysis using isotopic tracers and real-time biosensors for metabolites like lactate and ATP during serial tumor challenge co-cultures. This integrated platform directly tests which specific flux alterations are necessary and sufficient for enhanced anti-tumor potency and longevity, identifying high-value metabolic targets for therapeutic intervention.

ideas.title: Metabolically Adaptive CAR-T Cells for Heterogeneous Solid Tumors
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond blanket application of MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells by first characterizing the metabolic constraints of the tumor microenvironment in a panel of solid tumors (e.g., pancreatic, glioblastoma, ovarian). Based on this profiling, a modular engineering platform is used to generate CAR-T cells with metabolic programs optimized for each context, which may include MCJ silencing for oxidative tumors or alternative pathways for hypoxic niches. The system is validated using patient-derived organoids and in vivo models of each cancer type to ensure the chosen metabolic intervention enhances persistence, infiltration, and cytotoxicity. This strategy ensures therapeutic efficacy is not undermined by the diverse and hostile metabolic landscapes found across different solid malignancies.

ideas.title: Applying MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells to solid tumors using a 'tumor microenvironment normalization' strategy
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from civil engineering's soil stabilization techniques to the challenge of using MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells in solid tumors. In geotechnical engineering, unstable ground is treated with methods like grouting or electrokinetics to create a stable foundation for construction. Similarly, for solid tumors beyond leukemia and melanoma, the proposed research would develop combination strategies that first 'normalize' the hostile tumor microenvironment. This involves using pharmacological or physical interventions to reduce immunosuppression, hypoxia, and extracellular matrix density, thereby creating a more permissive and supportive 'foundation' that allows the metabolically enhanced, MCJ-silenced CAR-T cells to successfully infiltrate, persist, and exert their anti-tumor activity in a wider range of solid cancers.

ideas.title: Metabolic Flux-Guided DNA Origami to Reprogram Immunometabolism in Engineered T Cells
ideas.description: This project fuses DNA origami engineering with metabolic flux analysis to develop a new class of immunometabolic tools. We will design DNA nanostructures that are not just passive cGAS probes, but are functionalized to sense key metabolites (e.g., ATP/ADP ratio, lactate) and respond by changing conformation to release precise cytosolic DNA signals. By introducing these 'metabolite-responsive nanodevices' into MCJ-deficient CAR-T cells undergoing detailed flux analysis, we can directly correlate specific metabolic states with the activation of defined innate immune pathways (like cGAS-STING). This allows us to actively reprogram T cell metabolism by triggering tailored immunostimulatory or inhibitory DNA signals in response to real-time metabolic readouts, moving beyond observation to dynamic control.

ideas.title: Mechanistically dissect nucleosome-dependent inhibition of cGAS using a reconstituted synthetic chromatin system
ideas.description: This project will define the precise molecular rules by which chromatin packaging silences the cGAS DNA sensor to prevent autoimmune activation. We will reconstitute minimal nucleosomal arrays in vitro with defined histone modifications and challenge them with wild-type and engineered cGAS proteins to quantify binding and enzymatic activity. This synthetic system isolates the chromatin-cGAS interaction from other cellular regulatory factors, allowing us to test specific hypotheses about the structural basis of inhibition. The outcome will be a causal, biophysical model for this fundamental checkpoint, identifying potential vulnerabilities that could lead to disease if disrupted.

ideas.title: Mapping the Metabolic Bypass Network in MCJ-Silenced Cells
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that mitochondrial respiration is the sole determinant of efficacy in MCJ-targeted therapy by positing that other metabolic pathways can compensate. Drawing an analogy from metabolic engineering, where isotopic tracer-based flux analysis is used to map network rerouting after enzyme knockout, the adapted research idea is to apply metabolic flux analysis (MFA) to MCJ-silenced cells. Using stable isotope-labeled nutrients and mass spectrometry, the study will systematically quantify the flux through glycolysis, the pentose phosphate pathway, glutaminolysis, and fatty acid oxidation to identify and characterize the specific compensatory metabolic pathways that are activated, thereby defining the full metabolic bypass capacity that may dictate overall therapeutic success or failure.

ideas.title: Optogenetic, ΔΨm-gated mitophagy to selectively clear damaged mitochondria in CAR-T cells
ideas.description: This redesigned approach engineers CAR-T cells to express a light-activated mitophagy inducer (e.g., mito-KillerRed) fused to a ΔΨm-sensitive domain, ensuring it localizes specifically to depolarized mitochondria. Upon brief, controlled light exposure, reactive oxygen species are generated locally to trigger mitophagy exclusively in these tagged, dysfunctional organelles. This precision avoids the off-target loss of healthy mitochondria, preserving the metabolic capacity needed for antitumor function. The system thereby refreshes the mitochondrial pool only where needed, aiming to boost metabolic fitness and longevity without compromising baseline T cell energetics.

ideas.title: Direct coupling of single-cell cGAS enzymatic activity to transcriptional output via integrated activity-based profiling and sequencing
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond indirect transcriptional signatures to directly measure the fundamental driver of the pathway: cGAS enzymatic activity. A novel cell-permeable activity-based probe is used to covalently tag catalytically active cGAS enzyme in intact cells across tissues prior to dissociation. Single-cell libraries are then prepared to simultaneously quantify this probe-derived signal (via antibody-DNA barcoding) and the full transcriptional profile of each cell. This creates a definitive, causal link between the enzymatic trigger of the pathway and its downstream consequences, enabling the precise mapping of true cGAS-driven states versus bystander or STING-only activation across tissue microenvironments.

ideas.title: Inverse: Target MCJ in cancer cells using a 'Trojan Horse' delivery system
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of silencing MCJ in cancer cell mitochondria by incorporating a delivery strategy inspired by pest control. The analogous solution from agriculture involves using a targeted 'Trojan Horse' delivery system to introduce a biological agent specifically into a pest. Adapted to this cancer research context, the idea is to design a biomimetic carrier (e.g., a nanoparticle or engineered virus) that exploits the unique metabolic or surface features of cancer cells to gain entry and travel to the mitochondria. This carrier would then deploy its payload—molecular tools to silence the MCJ gene or protein—precisely where needed. This approach aims to increase the specificity and efficacy of inducing therapeutic oxidative stress within cancer cells while protecting healthy tissues, thereby sensitizing the tumor to subsequent radiation or chemotherapy.

ideas.title: Mapping developmentally programmed chromatin accessibility transitions as physiological triggers for cGAS sensing
ideas.description: This project investigates whether programmed, large-scale chromatin remodeling during key developmental transitions—such as zygotic genome activation, lineage commitment, or massive gene expression shifts—creates transient windows of nucleosome-free or structurally unusual DNA that can bypass nucleosomal inhibition to activate cGAS. We will employ embryonic stem cell differentiation models and cerebral organoid development, using acute degradation of specific chromatin remodelers to stall these transitions and precise mapping of DNA accessibility and cGAS activation. This approach shifts the focus from 'normal DNA' to specific, regulated epigenetic states as potential physiological cGAS ligands, providing a mechanistic bridge between development and innate immune signaling.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Priming' Strategy from Microbial Ecology to Modulate MCJ in Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology's use of priming agents to activate dormant soil microbes for bioremediation, this idea adapts the 'biostimulation' strategy to immuno-metabolism. The analogous problem—activating a resident but dysfunctional population for a specific task—is solved by adding a targeted stimulant to the local environment. Adapted here, the research idea is to develop a systemically deliverable or locally injectable MCJ-modulating drug (e.g., a small-molecule inhibitor or nanoparticle-delivered siRNA). This 'priming' therapy would be administered to cancer patients to directly and transiently inhibit MCJ in tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes within the tumor, aiming to boost their mitochondrial respiration, reverse exhaustion, and enhance the efficacy of endogenous or adoptively transferred T-cell therapies without ex vivo genetic engineering.

ideas.title: Adapting Industrial Bioprocess Flux Analysis for MCJ-deficient CAR-T Cell Metabolism
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in Industrial Biotechnology—optimizing microbial fermentation for product yield—to the context of CAR-T cell therapy. In bioprocessing, dynamic metabolic flux analysis (MFA) integrated with real-time sensor data and computational modeling is used to map and control metabolic pathways to overcome bottlenecks. By applying this methodology, we can employ detailed, dynamic MFA to map precise, time-dependent changes in MCJ-deficient CAR-T cell metabolism. Using a controlled culture system with real-time metabolic sensors and adapting constraint-based models from microbial systems will allow us to identify specific metabolic flux rerouting and bottlenecks, providing a detailed map to inform strategies for enhancing CAR-T cell longevity and anti-tumor function.

ideas.title: Systematically map the 'dark matter' of intracellular DNA sensors using functional redundancy screens
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from astrophysics' dark matter detection to identify redundant intracellular DNA sensing pathways. Just as dark matter is mapped by its gravitational effects on visible systems rather than direct observation, we propose to map the 'dark matter' of DNA sensing by systematically screening for immune and cellular phenotypes (the 'gravitational effects') in cGAS/STING-ablated models. By applying diverse cytosolic DNA stimuli (varying length, structure, modification) and measuring redundant outputs (transcriptomics, phosphoproteomics, metabolomics) across immune cell types, this phenotypic mapping will infer the existence and logic of alternative, cooperative, or indirect DNA sensing mechanisms that constitute a broader, redundant immune surveillance network.

ideas.title: Transient Metabolic Priming via siRNA-Nanoparticle Delivery During CAR-T Expansion
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from regenerative medicine, where transient epigenetic priming is used to precondition stem cells. The analogous solution uses transient delivery of modulators (e.g., siRNA via nanoparticles) only during ex vivo culture. Adapted to CAR-T manufacturing, the research idea is to employ biodegradable lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) to deliver MCJ-targeting siRNA during the expansion phase. The LNPs provide a strong, dose-controlled silencing effect that is washed away with the culture media, preconditioning the T cells for improved mitochondrial function and in vivo persistence without any permanent genetic change. This approach leverages scalable nanoparticle formulation technology to create a transient metabolic modulation step within existing CAR-T manufacturing workflows.

ideas.title: CRISPRa-mediated mitochondrial gene upregulation to probe cGAS sensing of physiological nuclear DNA damage
ideas.description: This project combines the tools of Idea B (CRISPRa for mitochondrial gene upregulation) with the core question of Idea A (cGAS's role in sensing normal cellular DNA). We will use CRISPRa to specifically upregulate nuclear-encoded mitochondrial Complex I genes, aiming to enhance mitochondrial function and potentially increase reactive oxygen species (ROS) as a downstream consequence. This controlled, endogenous perturbation is predicted to cause low-level nuclear DNA damage, providing a physiological source of self-DNA in the cytosol. We can then rigorously test if cGAS is activated by this normal cellular process, distinguishing its role in metabolic/developmental surveillance from its canonical function in pathogen detection.

ideas.title: Logic-Gated, Coordinated CRISPRa of Complex I Assembly with Functional Feedback
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple upregulation of individual genes to a systems-level activation strategy. A synthetic gene circuit coordinates the simultaneous CRISPRa-mediated transcription of a curated module of essential Complex I structural genes and their dedicated assembly factors (e.g., NDUFAF2, NDUFAF4) to promote correct biogenesis. The system integrates a ΔΨm biosensor (e.g., a FRET-based reporter) whose output regulates the CRISPRa machinery, creating a closed feedback loop that only sustains activation when mitochondrial function improves, thereby preventing harmful stoichiometric imbalances.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Epitope Mapping to Map cGAS Activation Landscapes with DNA Origami
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of protein epitope mapping from structural immunology to the problem of defining cGAS specificity. In epitope mapping, libraries of antigen variants pinpoint essential residues for antibody binding. Here, we replace the antigen library with a systematically varied library of DNA origami nanostructures. Each nanostructure acts as a defined 'mutant' of a generic cytosolic DNA signal, precisely controlling parameters such as bend angle, junction topology, duplex length, and inter-duplex distance. By assaying cGAS activation (via STING phosphorylation or IFN production) in response to this library, we can generate a high-resolution 'activation landscape' map. This map will identify the minimal structural determinants—the 'structural epitope'—that cGAS recognizes, moving beyond simple length measurements to define the precise shape and topology of immunostimulatory DNA.

ideas.title: Adapting pH-responsive polymer micelles for transient MCJ inhibition
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from oncology drug delivery—pH-responsive polymer micelles for targeted chemotherapy release—to the problem of transient MCJ inhibition. In cancer therapy, block copolymer micelles are engineered to disassemble and release drugs specifically in the acidic tumor microenvironment. We propose to design similarly pH-responsive, biodegradable nanoparticles that encapsulate MCJ-inhibiting agents (e.g., siRNA or peptides). These nanoparticles would be stable at physiological pH (7.4) but rapidly degrade and release their payload in the slightly acidic microenvironment characteristic of metabolically stressed or ischemic tissues (pH ~6.5-7.0), such as in heart failure or fatty liver disease. This allows for transient, spatially controlled MCJ silencing at the site of metabolic dysfunction, providing a therapeutic window for metabolic reprogramming without permanent genetic alteration, and the biodegradable polymers ensure clearance after payload delivery.

ideas.title: Apply cryo-EM and time-resolved mixing to visualize cGAS-DNA complex dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural enzymology—specifically, the use of time-resolved cryo-EM to visualize transient intermediates in systems like ribosomes and GPCRs—to the problem of visualizing cGAS-DNA complex dynamics. The analogous solution involves rapid mixing of purified components followed by plunge-freezing at controlled time points to trap successive conformational states. Adapted to this context, purified cGAS will be mixed with immunostimulatory DNA in a microfluidic device and plunge-frozen at millisecond to second intervals. Applying high-resolution cryo-EM to these time-point samples will enable the direct visualization and reconstruction of the structural trajectory of cGAS during DNA binding, oligomerization, and catalytic activation, providing an atomic-level movie of this critical innate immune sensing event.

ideas.title: Apply Phage-Assisted Continuous Evolution (PACE) to evolve cGAS biosensors
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using directed evolution to create cGAS-based biosensors. By drawing an analogy from the field of Protein Engineering, we adapt the solution of Phage-Assisted Continuous Evolution (PACE). In PACE, a desired protein function is linked to phage survival, enabling ultra-fast, hands-off evolution. We adapt this by engineering a PACE system where the activity of an evolving cGAS variant—specifically, its production of a nucleotide second messenger (e.g., cGAMP) in response to a target ligand—is coupled to the expression of a gene (like pIII) essential for bacteriophage infectivity. This creates a powerful continuous evolution platform to rapidly generate cGAS biosensors with novel input specificities (e.g., for pathogen DNA, mitochondrial DNA, or synthetic nucleic acid analogs) or optimized output characteristics for integration into diagnostic or synthetic biology circuits.

ideas.title: Programmable cytosolic delivery and assembly of DNA nanostructures to probe cGAS specificity
ideas.description: This approach bypasses the critical delivery barrier of pre-assembled DNA origami by encoding structural information within a compact, deliverable DNA construct. The construct is packaged into a non-inflammatory delivery vehicle, such as a designed ankyrin repeat protein (DARPin)-CPP fusion or a engineered viral-like particle, for efficient cytosolic entry. Once in the cytosol, the construct is released and, through toehold-mediated strand displacement or enzymatic ligation, folds into the precise DNA nanostructure designed to probe cGAS. This enables the study of cGAS activation by defined DNA shapes and lengths in their native cytosolic environment without the confounding effects of harsh delivery methods.
